EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accelerated snowmelt from a near-record snowpack due to above normal temperatures produced high and fast flow on drainages in La Plata County. As a result, flooding compromised The Oxbow Preserve along the Animas River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures increased snowmelt and caused flooding at the Oxbow Preserve along the Animas River, which led to its closure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving midlevel low pressure system lifted across southwest Colorado. This resulted in moderate snowfall for the Northwest San Juan Mountains, focused at elevations above 9500 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 9 inches of snow fell at elevations above 9500 feet. A trained spotter reported a locally higher amount of 11 inches near Trout Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accelerated snowmelt from a near-record snowpack due to above normal temperatures produced high and fast flow on the Colorado River. One such area was Glenwood Canyon, where an individual drowned in a rafting accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Colorado River in Glenwood Canyon ran high and fast as a result of accelerated snowmelt. While rafting in Glenwood Canyon, two rafters fell into the river while navigating a rapid and eddy. Both men were pulled from the water and brought back to shore. The rafting party began CPR on the two individuals - one rafter responded, but the other one did not.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Anomalously high moisture lingered over northwest Colorado. This, paired with daytime heating, led to scattered showers and thunderstorms. One such storm produced large hail up to an inch in diameter near Rangely.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail estimated to be one inch in diameter fell near Rangely.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture increased across western Colorado ahead of the next Pacific system. This, paired with daytime heating, led to scattered showers and thunderstorms. The stronger storms produced gusty winds in excess of 58 mph across the Grand Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 61 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Grand Junction Regional Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report of 3.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous supercell thunderstorms moved across parts of Logan, Morgan, Phillips, Weld and Washington counties during the evening and overnight, dropping baseball size hail or larger on several communities. Towns that received very large hail (baseball size or larger) included:  Akron, Iliff, Brush, Fort Morgan, Haxtun, Fleming, and Otis.  In addition to the large hail, an 82 mph wind gust was reported 6 SE of Holyoke and 81 mph wind gust was measured 8 S of Holyoke.  One large tree was downed by the wind.  Numerous reports of broken windows to homes along with extensive car damage.  Two tornadoes briefly touched down, but no damage was observed.

Severe thunderstorms produced large hail across parts of Elbert and Lincoln counties, along with one brief tornado.  The hail ranged from quarter to around tennis ball size.  Some of the roadways were completely covered by several inches of hail accumulation.  The tornado touched down in an open field with no damage observed. Localized flash flooding was observed near Boyero.

Very heavy rain occurred across some areas of northeast Colorado from June 11th until the early hours of June 12th with flash flooding in some areas.  The worst areas were south and southeast of Fort Collins towards Timnath and Windsor and in the Milliken area.  Locations in these areas received from 3.0 to  5.5 inches of rain.  Another area was located in and near Limon where significant flash flooding occurred along with a few water rescues.  Several roads were closed due to flash flooding north of CO-86 and west of I-70. Rainfall amounts in the these areas ranged from 2.4 to 3.7 inches. Severe thunderstorms produced large hail, from quarter to ping pong ball size.  The hail piled anywhere from 4 to 8 inches deep along roads and highways in Elbert and Lincoln counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 150 knot jetstream settled overhead across western Colorado as a Pacific trough approached the region. These stronger winds mixed down to the surface with some gusts meeting high wind criteria, or 75 mph, in some mountain areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from a decaying atmospheric river event paired with the passage of a strong cold front resulted in moderate to heavy snow across much of western Colorado. In addition, gusty winds led to significantly reduced visibility in blowing and drifting snow. Winds gusted in excess of 58 mph at times in the valleys, and were stronger than 80 mph in some mountain areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 7 to 14 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted to 60 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally strong and cold low pressure system dropped into the Great Basin and lifted across the Western Slope. Ample cold air and moisture paired with favorable orographics resulted in moderate to heavy snow for most of the mountains and valleys across western Colorado. Additionally, winds gusts exceeded 45 mph at times in the mountains and were greater than 30 mph in the valleys during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 7 to 14 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 40 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally strong and cold low pressure system dropped into the Great Basin and lifted across the Western Slope. Ample cold air and moisture paired with favorable orographics resulted in moderate to heavy snow for most of the mountains and valleys across western Colorado. Additionally, winds gusts exceeded 45 mph at times in the mountains and were greater than 30 mph in the valleys during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 40 mph at times.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds increased as yet another upper level trough and associated cold front dropped into the Great Basin. This led to some stronger winds across west-central Colorado. Some stronger gusts met high wind criteria, or 58 mph, in the Debeque to Silt Corridor and the Central Gunnison and Uncompahgre River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 59 MPH was measured at the Jay RAWS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed through western Colorado and produced moderate snow across the central mountains and some valleys. Additionally, gusty winds occurred with winds exceeding 70 mph in the mountains at times and in excess of 60 mph in the valleys, resulting in reduced visibilities. Instability associated with the front led to stronger snow bands along portions of west-central Colorado. As a result, a long-lived snow squall impacted the I-70 corridor from Grand Junction east towards Vail as visibilities dropped to less than one quarter mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 2 to 4 inches of snow fell across the area as winds gusted up to 55 mph at times. A snow squall moved through the area in the evening of the 22nd, dropping visibilities to less than half a mile as winds gusted to 40 mph with the squall at the Eagle Regional Airport. Reduced visibilities and gusty winds were also reported in Basalt.

CZ_NAME: LARIMER COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / NW WELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet winter storm produced widespread blowing and drifting snow across much of the urban corridor and northeast plains of Colorado.  Much of the region received a prolonged period of light to moderate snow, with bands of heavy wet snow from the 17th through the 18th.  At Denver International Airport, 9.2 inches of snowfall was observed along with 0.87 inches of precipitation.  This was the 8th largest 2-day snow event for Denver in the month of January to date.  The greatest impacts occurred east and northeast of Denver.   Across much of the urban corridor and northeast plains, storm totals ranged from from 3 to 12 inches, with 4 to 9 inches in the mountains and foothills.  Warming shelters were opened up in Denver and several school districts across the region were closed.  At Denver International Airport, there were 263 flight cancellations and 134 delayed flights.||Snow and blowing snow caused whiteout  conditions, along with drifting snow up to 3 feet deep.  Along the Interstate 70 corridor east of Denver to the Kansas state line, several 20 to 80 car pile ups were reported.  Numerous road closures were implemented across the northeast plains of Colorado, outside of the metro areas. The closures included:  Interstate 76 from Sterling to Nebraska state line;  CO 14 East of US Highway 85, US 138 Sterling to Julesburg, US 6 from Sterling to Nebraska state line, CO 52 from Fort Morgan to CO 14, CO 113 north of Iliff, CO 55 near Crook, CO 59 Sedgwick to Yuma, CO 385 Nebraska state line and Julesburg to Wray, CO 23 Holyoke to Nebraska state line, CO 11 north of Julesburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included 8 inches in Loveland, with 5 inches in Fort Collins.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system moved east over East-Central Colorado producing freezing fog, freezing rain, and heavy snow over Kit Carson and Yuma counties. Visibilities as low as one-quarter mile was observed in both counties with zero accidents reported. Both Kit Carson and Yuma counties had snow reports up to six inches over the western portions of the counties. Key impacts: reduced visibility, heavy snow, and hazardous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a period of 27 hours, Yuma County experienced freezing fog, freezing rain, and heavy snow. During the day on the 2nd, the fog produced visibilities as low as one-quarter mile with moisture from the fog and following freezing rain creating ice accumulations up to 0.08 inches. The greatest ice accumulations occurred near Wray, Colorado. During the evening hours through the early morning hours on the 3rd, heavy snow produced two to six inches of snow over the county. The highest snow amounts were reported on the west side of the county in and near the town of Yuma, Colorado. Key impacts: reduced visibility, heavy snow, and hazardous travel conditions.

At Denver International Airport, 9.2 inches of snowfall was observed along with 0.87 inches of precipitation.  This was the 8th largest 2-day snow event for Denver in the month of January to date.  The greatest impacts occurred east and northeast of Denver.   Across much of the urban corridor and northeast plains, storm totals ranged from from 3 to 12 inches, with 4 to 9 inches in the mountains and foothills.  Warming shelters were opened up in Denver and several school districts across the region were closed.  At Denver International Airport, there were 263 flight cancellations and 134 delayed flights.||Snow and blowing snow caused whiteout  conditions, along with drifting snow up to 3 feet deep.  Along the Interstate 70 corridor east of Denver to the Kansas state line, several 20 to 80 car pile ups were reported.  Numerous road closures were implemented across the northeast plains of Colorado, outside of the metro areas. The closures included:  Interstate 76 from Sterling to Nebraska state line;  CO 14 East of US Highway 85, US 138 Sterling to Julesburg, US 6 from Sterling to Nebraska state line, CO 52 from Fort Morgan to CO 14, CO 113 north of Iliff, CO 55 near Crook, CO 59 Sedgwick to Yuma, CO 385 Nebraska state line and Julesburg to Wray, CO 23 Holyoke to Nebraska state line, CO 11 north of Julesburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included 6 to 10.5 inches in and around Holyoke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system moved east over East-Central Colorado producing freezing fog, freezing rain, and heavy snow over Kit Carson and Yuma counties. Visibilities as low as one-quarter mile was observed in both counties with zero accidents reported. Both Kit Carson and Yuma counties had snow reports up to six inches over the western portions of the counties. Key impacts: reduced visibility, heavy snow, and hazardous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a period of 27 hours, Kit Carson County experienced freezing fog, freezing rain, and heavy snow. During the day on the 2nd, the fog produced visibilities as low as one-quarter mile with moisture from the fog and following freezing rain creating ice accumulations up to 0.02 inches. The greatest ice accumulations occurred near Burlington, Colorado. During the evening hours through the early morning hours on the 3rd, heavy snow produced two to six inches of snow over the county. The highest snow amounts were reported on the west side of the county near the town of Flagler, Colorado. Key impacts: reduced visibility, heavy snow, and hazardous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of January 30th and lasting into the morning of January 31st, portions of East-Central Colorado saw cold temperatures as low as -14 degrees Fahrenheit and westerly winds gusting up to 11 mph. These conditions allowed for extremely cold wind chill values as low as -33 degrees Fahrenheit within the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values at -25 degrees Fahrenheit or below were recorded across Cheyenne county. The lowest wind chill value of -29 degrees Fahrenheit was recorded at a Mesonet station 4 miles southwest of Arapahoe, Colorado at 1:15 AM MST. Key Impacts: extreme cold, frostbite.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two snowmobilers were caught, buried, and killed in a large avalanche on the east face of Mount Epworth near Corona Pass, about 6 miles east of Winter Park. Another group in the area was able to locate one of the riders with a transceiver. They performed CPR, but their efforts were unsuccessful. The second rider was not wearing an avalanche transceiver. Grand County Sheriff's Deputies and Grand County search and rescue were unable to locate the second rider before dark. They continued the search the following day and the second victim was recovered.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two snowmobilers were caught, buried and killed by a large avalanche.

CZ_NAME: WEST JACKSON & WEST GRAND COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet winter storm produced widespread blowing and drifting snow across much of the urban corridor and northeast plains of Colorado.  Much of the region received a prolonged period of light to moderate snow, with bands of heavy wet snow from the 17th through the 18th.  At Denver International Airport, 9.2 inches of snowfall was observed along with 0.87 inches of precipitation.  This was the 8th largest 2-day snow event for Denver in the month of January to date.  The greatest impacts occurred east and northeast of Denver.   Across much of the urban corridor and northeast plains, storm totals ranged from from 3 to 12 inches, with 4 to 9 inches in the mountains and foothills.  Warming shelters were opened up in Denver and several school districts across the region were closed.  At Denver International Airport, there were 263 flight cancellations and 134 delayed flights.||Snow and blowing snow caused whiteout  conditions, along with drifting snow up to 3 feet deep.  Along the Interstate 70 corridor east of Denver to the Kansas state line, several 20 to 80 car pile ups were reported.  Numerous road closures were implemented across the northeast plains of Colorado, outside of the metro areas. The closures included:  Interstate 76 from Sterling to Nebraska state line;  CO 14 East of US Highway 85, US 138 Sterling to Julesburg, US 6 from Sterling to Nebraska state line, CO 52 from Fort Morgan to CO 14, CO 113 north of Iliff, CO 55 near Crook, CO 59 Sedgwick to Yuma, CO 385 Nebraska state line and Julesburg to Wray, CO 23 Holyoke to Nebraska state line, CO 11 north of Julesburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals ranged from 5 to 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system brought heavy snow to East Central Colorado in mid January.  Snowfall amounts ranged from an inch or less in northern Yuma County to over eleven inches in Cheyenne County.  This event was unusual in that there was very little blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 in the western part of the county to 10 in the eastern part of the county.  The highest snow report was near Burlington.||Key Impacts:  snow packed roadways, limited visibility.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 in the western part of the county to 11.5 in the eastern part of the county.  The highest snow report was near Cheyenne Wells.||Key Impacts:  snow packed roadways, limited visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of January 30th and lasting into the morning of January 31st, portions of East-Central Colorado saw cold temperatures as low as -14 degrees Fahrenheit and westerly winds gusting up to 11 mph. These conditions allowed for extremely cold wind chill values as low as -33 degrees Fahrenheit within the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values at -25 degrees Fahrenheit or below were recorded across Kit Carson county. The lowest wind chill value of -33 degrees Fahrenheit was recorded at a Mesonet station 17 miles north of Flagler, Colorado at 7:42 PM MST.||Key Impacts:  extreme cold, frostbite.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the evening hours of January 17th, a low-pressure system from southeast Colorado traveled northeast across northwest Kansas and on into south central Nebraska. As this system trekked through the region, an initial snowfall occurred on the leading edge of the system dropping up to 5 inches of snow overnight on the 17th. During the day on the 18th, precipitation began to wrap around the system and create additional snowfall. In Yuma county Colorado, a foot of snow was the maximum snowfall report in northeast Colorado. Interstate 70 was also shut down for a few hours from the Colorado/Kansas border to Denver due to safety concerns. I-70 was closed in both directions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 10:10 AM on the 18th, the public reported 8 inches of snowfall since the start of the storm. The night before, at 8:40 PM, law enforcement reported 0.12 inches of freezing rain that was causing hazardous travel. The highest snowfall report of 12 inches was received from the public at 7:15 PM on the 18th. Winds at both the Yuma and Wray AWOS sites reported gusts over 20 mph which resulted in blowing snow. The blowing snow reduced visibility under 1 mile on occasion. Key impacts: heavy snow, blowing snow, snow packed roads and icy roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values at -25 degrees Fahrenheit or below were recorded in across Yuma county. The lowest wind chill value of -31 degrees Fahrenheit was recorded at a Mesonet station in Kirk, Colorado at 8:45 PM MST.||Key Impacts:  extreme cold, frostbite.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near 1:40 AM on the 18th, the Colorado Department of Transportation closed Interstate 70 through Kit Carson county due to safety concerns. A maximum snowfall report of 10 |inches was sent in by the public 7 AM on the 19th. Multiple reports across the county had reports of over 6 inches of snow from this event. The Burlington ASOS reported gusts up to 37 mph during the winter storm while it was snowing. Reduced visibility down to 0.5 mile due to blowing snow at the ASOS site, however it is likely that lower visibilities occurred and were not reported. Key impacts: heavy snow, blowing snow, snow packed roads.

CZ_NAME: FOUR CORNERS / UPPER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from a decaying atmospheric river event paired with the passage of a strong cold front resulted in moderate to heavy snow across much of western Colorado. In addition, gusty winds led to significantly reduced visibility in blowing and drifting snow. Winds gusted in excess of 58 mph at times in the valleys, and were stronger than 80 mph in some mountain areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area with up to 12 inches reported in Dolores. Winds gusted up to 56 mph at times. There was a power outage for over five hours in Dove Creek due to the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and evening of April 19th, a cold front passed through East-Central Colorado producing strong and damaging wind gusts in the area. High wind gusts up to 67 mph were recorded in the region. Blowing dust was also reported in the region due to these high winds that reduced visibilities down to one-quarter of a statute mile in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibilities down to one-quarter of a statute mile was reported within Kit Carson County due to high winds causing blowing dust. The lowest visibility was seen on a Viaero weather camera located 1 mile east of Burlington, CO that showed visibility from around one-quarter to one-half of a statute mile due to blowing dust in the area. Nearby Mesonet station estimated wind gusts up to 49 mph during this time. Time range estimated using nearby KITR ASOS observations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms brought heavy, wind swept snow, to the mountains of southwest Colorado from mid to late February. This snow fell on an older layer of snow that was deposited in late January and early February, which had become more faceted and weak. As a result, an avalanche was triggered south of Vallecito Reservoir.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two backcountry skiers unintentionally triggered an avalanche on a northwest-facing slope near Vallecito Reservoir. The avalanche was 12 to 30 inches deep and about 200 feet wide at the crown face. It ran about 300 vertical feet through a burn scar, stumps, small aspens, and thick willows. Both of the skiers were buried and did not survive. The time of the avalanche is unknown, but occurred sometime during the mid morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second shortwave disturbance tracked across the Western Slope. Ample moisture paired with favorable orographics and support aloft resulted in moderate snow across the mountains of western Colorado. Winds in both the mountains and valleys exceeded 50 mph at times. Additionally, stronger snow bands and subsequent snow squalls developed along portions of I-70 and Highway 40, with visibilities dropping to less than one quarter of a mile in heavy snow and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 50 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of moderate to heavy snow showers coupled with strong winds produced poor visibilities and hazardous driving conditions in the mountains.  Snow squalls impacts parts of the Interstate 70 corridor west of the Eisenhower Tunnel, as well as State Highway 40 west of Kremmeling.  Storm totals in the mountains and foothills ranged from 4 to 7 inches in most areas with up to 14 inches observed near Silverthorne.  Along the urban corridor and northeast plains, strorm totals ranged from 3 to 6 inches west of Interstate 25, with amounts ranging from 1 to 5 inches elsewhere.  Wind chill temperatures on the morning of the 23rd, ranged anywhere from -25F to -35F across the urban corridor and northeast plains.  Several locations established new record low temperatures for the date including Denver.  In Denver, record low temperatures of -7F and -11F were set on the 22nd and 23rd, with a record low maximum of 7 on the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals ranged from 2 to 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds increased as yet another upper level trough and associated cold front dropped into the Great Basin. This led to some stronger winds across northwest Colorado. Some stronger sustained wind speeds met high wind criteria, or 40 mph for one or more hours, in the Lower Yampa River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were sustained at 40 mph for an hour at the Rangely Airport. Additionally, Colorado Highway 40 was closed for several hours due to impacts from blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A north-south oriented snow squall developed off the Palmer divide to extend across portions of East-Central Colorado moving east-northeast to the Kansas border of Yuma, Kit Carson, and Cheyenne counties over a span of up to two hours. The snow squall created visibility reductions ranging as low as one-quarter mile to 100 yards, which mainly impacted travel across I-70, US-40, and US-36. Snow reports ranged from a trace to one inch of snow across the three counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall. A band of heavy snowfall extending across portions of East-Central Colorado moved east-northeast across Yuma County over a span of an hour. The snow squall created visibility reductions as low as one-quarter mile mainly near the city of Yuma with reports up to one inch of snow. Key impacts: visibility reductions, hazardous travel, heavy snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In mid February heavy snow occurred across East Central Colorado, along with wind gusts around 30 MPH.  Snow amounts ranged from a couple inches to almost 10 inches.  The highest snow amounts occurred in southern Yuma and eastern Kit Carson counties.  The combined effect of the wind and snow yielded drifts of two to four feet and visibility of less than a half mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from around six inches in the west to almost 10 inches in the eastern part of the county.||Key impacts:  blowing snow, drifting snow, limited visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous supercell thunderstorms moved across parts of Logan, Morgan, Phillips, Weld and Washington counties during the evening and overnight, dropping baseball size hail or larger on several communities. Towns that received very large hail (baseball size or larger) included:  Akron, Iliff, Brush, Fort Morgan, Haxtun, Fleming, and Otis.  In addition to the large hail, an 82 mph wind gust was reported 6 SE of Holyoke and 81 mph wind gust was measured 8 S of Holyoke.  One large tree was downed by the wind.  Numerous reports of broken windows to homes along with extensive car damage.  Two tornadoes briefly touched down, but no damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed through western Colorado and produced a quick shot of moderate snow across the northern mountains as well as some central ranges. Additionally, gusty winds occurred with winds exceeding 50 mph in the mountains at times, resulting in reduced visibilities. Instability associated with the front led to stronger snow bands along portions of northwest Colorado. Wind gusts exceeded 40 mph with a snow squall that impacted portions of Rio Blanco County as visibilities dropped to less than one quarter mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 45 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from a decaying atmospheric river event paired with the passage of a strong cold front resulted in moderate to heavy snow across much of western Colorado. In addition, gusty winds led to significantly reduced visibility in blowing and drifting snow. Winds gusted in excess of 58 mph at times in the valleys, and were stronger than 80 mph in some mountain areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 2 to 4 inches of snow fell across the zone. Winds gusted in excess of 50 mph at times, which resulted in visibility of less than 1/4 mile at times.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed through western Colorado and produced moderate snow across the central mountains and some valleys. Additionally, gusty winds occurred with winds exceeding 70 mph in the mountains at times and in excess of 60 mph in the valleys, resulting in reduced visibilities. Instability associated with the front led to stronger snow bands along portions of west-central Colorado. As a result, a long-lived snow squall impacted the I-70 corridor from Grand Junction east towards Vail as visibilities dropped to less than one quarter mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area as winds gusted up to 70 mph at times. A snow squall moved through the area in the evening of the 22nd, dropping visibilities to less than one quarter of a mile with the squall in Vail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle and freezing fog occurred within East-Central Colorado on the evening of March 8th going into the following morning of March 9th. The resulting icy roads and reduced visibilities down to 150 ft. created hazardous driving conditions that led to vehicular accidents within the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle and freezing fog was reported within Cheyenne county creating low visibilities down to 150 ft. and icy roads. Due to these hazardous driving conditions, an accident occurred between County Road K and County Road L at Mile Marker 132. No injuries were reported. Key impacts: hazardous traveling conditions, road closures, vehicular accidents.

CZ_NAME: ELBERT / C & E DOUGLAS COUNTIES ABOVE 6000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought a period of moderate to heavy snowfall to portions of the urban corridor and northeast plains.  The heaviest snowfall occurred over eastern Larimer and northeast Boulder counties early in the morning, then shifted into the northeast plains of Colorado for much of the day.  Snowfall rates in some up those bands were as high as 2 inches per hour.  Storm totals generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches.  The heaviest snowfall occurred in Weld County where Pierce picked up an impressive 13.5 inches of snow.  Gusty northerly winds 35 to 45 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow over the northeast plains.  The combination of snow and gusty winds caused several road closures near the Nebraska border where several roads were closed including Interstate 76 due to blowing snow and poor visibilities.||Stronger gusts to 58 mph were noted across northern Lincoln County, with areas of light snow producing low visibilities along the I-70 corridor east of Denver to Limon at times.  Storm totals in and near the Palmer Divide, ranged from 1 to 3 inches.  Peak wind gusts included:  62 mph at Clifford, 55 mph at Cedar Point, 53 mph at Limon Municipal Airport and 52 mph in Hugo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals ranged from 1 to 3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific system dug into the Great Basin from California, resulting in a tightening southwesterly flow gradient across the Western Slope. Winds gusted from 55 to 65 mph at times in some of the higher elevations. Additionally, an influx in moisture paired with the progression of a cold front resulted in moderate to heavy snow across the mountains of western Colorado as the Pacific storm lifted overhead.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 7 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 48 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system produced a brief period of moderate to heavy snowfall that primarily impacted the foothills of Jefferson County.  The more impressive storm totals included:  14 inches, 5.4 miles northwest of Evergreen, 10 inches near Conifer and Crescent Village, with 8 to 9 inches at Genesee, 9 miles west-northwest of Golden and near Pinecliffe.  Storm totals across the rest of the Front Range mountains, foothills and South Park ranged from 3 to 6 inches.

CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of heavy snowfall developed over the mountains, foothills and adjacent plains of Boulder and Larimer counties.  Multiple accidents occurred during the morning commute as the roadways became icy and slick. Storm totals included:  11.2 inches near Allenspark, 11 inches in Poudre Park and near Estes Park, 10.9 inches, 4 miles east-northeast of Nederland; 10 inches near Crescent Village, 6.5 inches, 5 miles south-southwest of Morrison; 6.2 inches, 12 miles northwest of Golden; with 5.6  inches at Genesee.  Along the I-25 corridor, storm totals ranged from 1 to 5 inches, heaviest from around Boulder north to Fort Collins.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals ranged from 8 to 11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered storms developed over Northeast Colorado, moving southeast across Yuma County. As the storms matured over the county they produced thunderstorm wind gusts up to 68 mph and localized areas of blowing dust. The strongest wind gust was reported near Idalia, CO where there was also some small tree limbs snapped off trees. Blowing dust was observed near Eckley, CO with visibility reductions as low as 100 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported estimated wind gusts up to 50 mph that caused blowing dust to occur as a thunderstorm moved over the location. Visibility was reduced to 100 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and evening of April 19th, a cold front passed through East-Central Colorado producing strong and damaging wind gusts in the area. High wind gusts up to 67 mph were recorded in the region. Blowing dust was also reported in the region due to these high winds that reduced visibilities down to one-quarter of a statute mile in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts of 58 mph or greater were observed in Kit Carson County. The highest wind gust recorded was 67 miles at the KITR ASOS located 4 miles south of Burlington, CO. These winds also lowered visibility down to one quarter of a statute mile near Burlington, CO. Damage caused by high winds included damage to a fence and windows on a trailer located 17 miles north of Flagler, CO along with equipment blown around and a gas tank on a stand being knocked over at the location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level jet settled over the Four Corners region ahead of an abnormally cold Pacific system. Windy to very windy conditions occurred across western Colorado ahead of the cold front. Some gusts met high wind criteria, or were greater than 58 mph in the valleys and greater than 75 mph in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 60 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Grand Junction Regional Airport. Another gust of 67 mph was reported by the automated weather station in Palisade. Several power outages occurred in the valley as a result of downed poles from the wind.

Increased moisture paired with orographic support resulted in moderate snow across the northern and central mountains in Western Colorado. Warm air out ahead of the front resulted in high snow levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures accelerated snowmelt from a near-record snowpack. This  resulted in high and fast flows on several rivers and drainages across Western Colorado. The increased runoff and flooding compromised several county roads and bridges across Montezuma, Archuleta, Routt, Delta, Dolores, Montrose, and Moffat counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapid snowmelt from the Dolores River led to various drainages breaching their banks. This resulted in flooded downtown residential buildings in the town of Dolores. The water also flooded streets, some yards, and school playgrounds. Flood waters did not recede for several days.

The increased runoff and flooding compromised several county roads and bridges across Montezuma, Archuleta, Routt, Delta, Dolores, Montrose, and Moffat counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pagosa Ranger District in the San Juan National Forest reported at least three instances of flooding on Piedra Road (CR 600) north of the Turkey Springs Trailhead. The flooding was the result of rapid runoff from snowmelt.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapid snowmelt resulted in portions of Dave Wood Road to be washed out from flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures accelerated snowmelt from a near-record snowpack. This  resulted in high and fast flows on several rivers and drainages across Western Colorado. The increased runoff and flooding compromised several county roads and bridges across Montezuma, Archuleta, Routt, Delta, Dolores, Montrose, and Moffat counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapid snowmelt resulted in flooding across Road 9.7 in Dolores County, as the Dolores River overran its banks. The road was closed for a prolonged period as flood waters did not recede for several days. The accelerated snowmelt and subsequent pooling of water flooded the local high school, which damaged the gym flooring.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures accelerated snowmelt from a near-record snowpack. This  resulted in high and fast flows on several rivers and drainages across Western Colorado. The increased runoff and flooding compromised several county roads and bridges across Montezuma, Archuleta, Routt, Delta, Dolores, Montrose, and Moffat counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapid snowmelt resulted in Dry Creek, which runs underneath Highway 40, coming out of its banks. As a result, Highway 40 was closed and shut down in both directions from Craig to Steamboat Springs due to safety concerns. Schools in Hayden were closed. Flood waters did not recede for several days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous supercell thunderstorms moved across parts of Logan, Morgan, Phillips, Weld and Washington counties during the evening and overnight, dropping baseball size hail or larger on several communities. Towns that received very large hail (baseball size or larger) included:  Akron, Iliff, Brush, Fort Morgan, Haxtun, Fleming, and Otis.  In addition to the large hail, an 82 mph wind gust was reported 6 SE of Holyoke and 81 mph wind gust was measured 8 S of Holyoke.  One large tree was downed by the wind.  Numerous reports of broken windows to homes along with extensive car damage.  Two tornadoes briefly touched down, but no damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accelerated snowmelt from a near-record snowpack due to above normal temperatures produced high and fast flow on drainages in Gunnison County. As a result, flooding compromised and led to the failure of a culvert which led to flooding near Bear Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A culvert failed due to excess runoff along Colorado Highway 133 in Bear Creek, or near mile marker 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty southwest winds occurred across western Colorado ahead of a strong cold front. Winds gusted from 40 to 45 mph for the lower elevations with gusts exceeding 55 mph in the mountains. Some gusts met high wind criteria, or were greater than 58 mph in the valleys and greater than 75 mph for the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 69 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Meeker Coulter Field Airport with a peak gust of 62 mph occurring at the Craig-Moffat Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of fast moving, weak showers in the afternoon of the 13th created high winds as they collapsed. Winds not associated with the showers were gusting to around 30-40 mph  and as the showers collapsed, short-lived gusts reached up to 66 mph in eastern Colorado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving disturbance trekked across northwest Colorado. Enough moisture paired with orographics and jet support resulted in moderate snow over the northern mountains as well as some high valleys. Winds gusted up to 30 mph in the valleys and in excess of 45 mph in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 54 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced  intense winds in the vicinities of Haxtun, Holyoke and Pleasant Valley with gusts ranging from 75 to 98 mph.  Also, hail up to nickel size was observed north of Haxtun.   East of Denver, blowing dust produced very low visibilities along Interstate 70, which resulted in multiple crashes during the brownouts.  Significant blowing dust was reported along I-70 between Watkins and Strasburg, with the worst conditions around Bennett.   A dry microburst near Elizabeth produced a peak wind gust to 59 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing dust produced very low visibilities along Interstate 70, which resulted in multiple crashes during the brownouts in eastern portions of Adams County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms developed southwest of Denver and pushed north-northeast through the metro area. The storms produced extensive hail, ranging from quarter to tennis ball size.  As the storms move northeast onto the plains, the hail increase in size from baseball to softball size.  In addition, the storm produced several tornadoes as they move across Morgan and Washington counties.   Southeast of Brush, an EF1 tornado touched down and caused extensive damage to a residence.  Part of the roof was torn off the residence. The nearby barn had a portion of its roof ripped off the building. Some wood storage buildings were destroyed and trees on the property were uprooted. The two elderly residence in the home at the time were not injured.  Several other weaker tornadoes touched down in Washington County but no damage was observed.

Heavy snow from a series of moist, atmospheric river events from late February into mid-March 2023 resulted in an unstable snowpack across the Colorado Rockies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three backcountry skiers triggered and were buried or partially buried by a large avalanche in the Maroon Bowl outside of Aspen Highlands Ski Area on a steep northwest-facing slope. The avalanche occurred at an elevation of 11500 feet and ran for 2000 vertical feet. Two skiers were able to extricate themselves, but the third perished under the weight of the snow and debris.

On the 16th a low pressure system moved over the Rockies bringing light snow and strong to high winds to Colorado Plains. During the early morning hours, high winds impacted eastern portions of Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties while a brief snow squall moved over the Kit Carson and Lincoln County lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of snow produced snow squall conditions near Flagler, Colorado with northerly winds gusting to 52 mph creating visibility reductions as low as one-quarter mile. Only trace amounts of snow were reported. Key impacts: hazardous travel, snow squall, visibility reductions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 16th a low pressure system moved over the Rockies bringing light snow and strong to high winds to Colorado Plains. During the early morning hours, high winds impacted eastern portions of Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties while a brief snow squall moved over the Kit Carson and Lincoln County lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong to high winds produced gusts from 40 to 60 mph out of the north across the eastern half of Kit Carson County within one hour. The strongest gusts of 58 and 60 mph were observed via the Kit Carson County Airport ASOS station. Key impacts: hazardous travel and high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high impact storm system moved across East Central Colorado. The storm system produced an environment that was favorable for any of the developing thunderstorms to produce a tornado, which led to several tornadoes occurring on this day.  The strongest tornado of the day was an EF 1 that formed in Cheyenne County Kansas, caused damage, and carried that damage into Yum County.||The largest hail reported was dime size at Cheyenne Wells.  The strongest wind gust was 65 MPH near Kirk.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail size ranged from pea to dime in size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving upper level disturbance brushed northwest Colorado. The northern mountains, favored in the northwesterly flow, received a quick shot of moderate snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 9 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving upper level disturbance brushed northwest Colorado. The northern mountains, favored in the northwesterly flow, received a quick shot of moderate snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of an upper level disturbance paired with a strong jet aloft resulted in a quick shot of snow across northwest Colorado. The northern mountains saw moderate accumulations in addition to gusty winds in excess of 60 mph at times. Heavy snow fell in portions of the Central Yampa River Basin when a band of snow dropped 6 to 10 inches in some areas in less than 12 hours. Winds also gusted up to 35 mph in the valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of up to 12 inches estimated at the Whiskey Park SNOTEL site. Winds gusted up to 60 mph at times.

Broad troughing overhead combined with cold air aloft and a strong March sun angle resulted in convectively driven scattered showers across the Western Slope. Stronger snow bands and subsequent snow squalls developed across some of the northern and central valleys, with visibilities dropping to less than one quarter of a mile in heavy snow and strong winds across portions of the Central Yampa River Basin and the Central Gunnison and Uncompahgre River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow squall impacted the Meeker area, reducing visibilities to less than one quarter of a mile for an extended period of time as it moved through. The Meeker Coulter Field Airport reported wind gusts up to 38 mph with the squall.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad troughing overhead combined with cold air aloft and a strong March sun angle resulted in convectively driven scattered showers across the Western Slope. Stronger snow bands and subsequent snow squalls developed across some of the northern and central valleys, with visibilities dropping to less than one quarter of a mile in heavy snow and strong winds across portions of the Central Yampa River Basin and the Central Gunnison and Uncompahgre River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow squall impacted the Montrose area, reducing visibilities to less than one quarter of a mile for around 30 minutes as it moved through. The Montrose Regional Airport reported wind gusts up to 33 mph with the squall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another atmospheric river impacted western Colorado, bringing an abundance of moisture and a cold front. This led to additional moderate to heavy snow in the mountains with mostly rain in the valleys. Winds gusted in excess of 45 mph at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 11 inches of snow fell at the Columbine Pass SNOTEL site, much of that in less than 12 hours.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another atmospheric river impacted western Colorado, bringing an abundance of moisture and a cold front. This led to additional moderate to heavy snow in the mountains with mostly rain in the valleys. Winds gusted in excess of 45 mph at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of up to 8 inches reported. Much of the snow fell in less than 12 hours. Winds gusted up to 50 mph at times.

Wind gusts around 60 MPH were observed during the day as a winter storm system moved through East Central Colorado.  The highest wind gust reported was 66 MPH at the Burlington airport.  Prior to the snow moving in the winds ahead of the snow caused blowing dust to reduce the visibility to between half and three quarters of a mile at Kit Carson.  Once the snow began to move in, a snow squall occurred in the Burlington area where the combined effect of heavy snow and gusty winds caused the visibility to fall to a quarter mile or less for a half hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall.  Wind gusts of 30 to 50 MPH combined with heavy snow to reduce the visibility to a quarter mile or less during this timeframe.||Key Impacts:  blowing snow, reduced visibility.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station just north of Flagler reported the high wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain occurred across some areas of northeast Colorado from June 11th until the early hours of June 12th with flash flooding in some areas.  The worst areas were south and southeast of Fort Collins towards Timnath and Windsor and in the Milliken area.  Locations in these areas received from 3.0 to  5.5 inches of rain.  Another area was located in and near Limon where significant flash flooding occurred along with a few water rescues.  Several roads were closed due to flash flooding north of CO-86 and west of I-70. Rainfall amounts in the these areas ranged from 2.4 to 3.7 inches. Severe thunderstorms produced large hail, from quarter to ping pong ball size.  The hail piled anywhere from 4 to 8 inches deep along roads and highways in Elbert and Lincoln counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused flooding and flash flooding in and around Fort Collins.  The worst areas were south and southeast of Fort Collins towards Timnath.  Locations in these areas received from 3 to 5 inches of rain.   Widespread street flooding with road closures were reported.  Numerous vehicles were stranded in standing water in excess of 6 inches in depth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of moist, atmospheric river events brough very heavy snow to much of the western U.S. including western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An avalanche caught three backcountry recreationists (two skiers, one splitboarder) in the upper Rapid Creek, southwest of the town of Marble, Colorado. Two members of the group were caught up in the avalanche and one was completely buried in over 4 feet of snow and debris. One member was injured and the third was completely buried and perished before rescue groups were able to locate them.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts up to 11 inches reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms developed southwest of Denver and pushed north-northeast through the metro area. The storms produced extensive hail, ranging from quarter to tennis ball size.  As the storms move northeast onto the plains, the hail increase in size from baseball to softball size.  In addition, the storm produced several tornadoes as they move across Morgan and Washington counties.   Southeast of Brush, an EF1 tornado touched down and caused extensive damage to a residence.  Part of the roof was torn off the residence. The nearby barn had a portion of its roof ripped off the building. Some wood storage buildings were destroyed and trees on the property were uprooted. The two elderly residence in the home at the time were not injured.  Several other weaker tornadoes touched down in Washington County but no damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 150 knot jetstream settled overhead across western Colorado as a Pacific trough approached the region. These stronger winds mixed down to the surface with some gusts meeting high wind criteria, or 75 mph, in some mountain areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated stations across the area reported wind gusts from 75 to 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture trapped under a strong temperatures inversion led to the development of dense fog across the Grand Valley in Western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog was observed across the Grand Valley including at the Grand Junction Regional Airport as visibility was reduced to less than one quarter of a mile for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level negatively-tilted shortwave trough lifted across the Western Slope. Residual moisture paired with a cold air mass aloft resulted in scattered snow showers over the mountains. Moderate accumulations fell in a short period of time across the northern and some central ranges.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 7 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of up to 11 inches estimated at the Tower SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought an abundance of moisture to the Western Slope. This, paired with the passage of an upper level trough across the Northern Rockies and associated cold front, resulted in moderate to heavy snow for the mountains. Upwards of 1 to 2 feet of snow fell on the favored slopes. Mild air associated with the atmospheric river kept snow levels high with mostly all rain or a rain/snow mix in the valleys. Winds gusted in excess of 60 mph at times in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 7 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts up to 15 inches estimated at the Tower SNOTEL site. Winds gusted up to 60 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific system dug into the Great Basin from California, resulting in a tightening southwesterly flow gradient across the Western Slope. Winds gusted from 55 to 65 mph at times in some of the higher elevations. Additionally, an influx in moisture paired with the south and eastward progression of a cold front resulted in moderate to heavy snow across the mountains of western Colorado as the Pacific storm lifted overhead.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 7 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system swept through eastern Colorado in the early hours of the 31st. The following pressure rise behind the low pressure system resulted in strong winds. The Burlington, Colorado airport reported a 14 millibar pressure rise over 12 hours. The highest recorded gust in eastern Colorado during this event was in Yuma county with a gust of 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum measured gust was from a Mesonet site 5 miles north-northwest of Heartstrong, Colorado. Another gust of 58 mph was recorded at the Yuma, Colorado airport.

Upwards of 1 to 2 feet of snow fell on the favored slopes. Mild air associated with the atmospheric river kept snow levels high with mostly all rain or a rain/snow mix in the valleys. Winds gusted in excess of 60 mph at times in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 8 to 18 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 66 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second in a series of Pacific disturbances progressed across northwest Colorado. Stronger snow bands and subsequent snow squalls associated with the system developed across the northern valleys, with visibilities dropping to less than one quarter of a mile in heavy snow and strong winds across portions of the Lower and Central Yampa River Basins.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow squall impacted the Craig area, reducing visibilities to less than one quarter of a mile for around 20 minutes as it moved through. The Craig-Moffat County Airport reported wind gusts up to 52 mph with the squall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms developed southwest of Denver and pushed north-northeast through the metro area. The storms produced extensive hail, ranging from quarter to tennis ball size.  As the storms move northeast onto the plains, the hail increase in size from baseball to softball size.  In addition, the storm produced several tornadoes as they move across Morgan and Washington counties.   Southeast of Brush, an EF1 tornado touched down and caused extensive damage to a residence.  Part of the roof was torn off the residence. The nearby barn had a portion of its roof ripped off the building. Some wood storage buildings were destroyed and trees on the property were uprooted. The two elderly residence in the home at the time were not injured.  Several other weaker tornadoes touched down in Washington County but no damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high impact storm system moved across East Central Colorado. The storm system produced an environment that was favorable for any of the developing thunderstorms to produce a tornado, which led to several tornadoes occurring on this day.  The strongest tornado of the day was an EF 1 that formed in Cheyenne County Kansas, caused damage, and carried that damage into Yum County.||The largest hail reported was dime size at Cheyenne Wells.  The strongest wind gust was 65 MPH near Kirk.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado remained mostly over open fields. There was a debris signature on radar, which was likely dust but there were some dead trees and sheet metal from an abandoned property that may have been lofted within Cheyenne County Kansas, but carried over into Yuma County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds increased as yet another upper level trough and associated cold front dropped into the Great Basin. Additionally, marginal instability resulted in stronger showers that produced gusty outflow winds. Some outflow gusts met high wind criteria, or were greater than 58 mph, in the Grand Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 69 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Grand Junction Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on snow resulted in rapid runoff across portions of southwest Colorado. This led to flooding in portions of Montezuma County when the combination of snowmelt and rain led to the failure of a culvert. Portions of Highway 184 closed due to the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapid snowmelt and rain caused flooding and the closure of Colorado Highway 184 near its intersection with U.S. Highway 491. Heavy rain and rapid snowmelt overwhelmed a culvert which failed, contributing to the backup of the culvert and subsequent flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter systems associated with an atmospheric river brought an abundance of heavy, wet snow to southwest Colorado. This, paired with periods of warmer temperatures in between the events, resulted in an unstable snowpack.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A father and two children were moving between a building and vehicle when they were caught in a roof avalanche. The force of the avalanche threw one child towards the building, where their airway was covered in debris but part of their head was visible (partially buried-critical). The other two were completely buried about 10 feet away, in close proximity to each other, and under two to three feet of avalanche debris. Bystanders and emergency services excavated the three. All three were taken to hospital care. One of the children later died from injuries sustained in the avalanche.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accelerated snowmelt from a near-record snowpack due to above normal temperatures produced high and fast flow on drainages in several counties across the Western Slope. As a result, flooding compromised various low-lying roads and areas. This included some lanes along I-70 west of Fruita, Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt produced a washout on National Forest System Road (NFSR) #525-Potter Basin road located at mile post 1.172 on the Ouray Ranger District, northeast of 25 Mesa Guard Station. The road was not passable and remained closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accelerated snowmelt from a near-record snowpack due to above normal temperatures and precipitation produced high and fast flow on drainages in Delta and Garfield counties. As a result, flooding compromised various low-lying recreational trails.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on Surface Creek led to the closure of a recreational trail along the river, near Cedaredge, as the trail was washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accelerated snowmelt from a near-record snowpack due to above normal temperatures produced high and fast flow on drainages in portions of San Miguel and Mesa counties. As a result, flooding and debris flows compromised Fall Creek Road as well as portions of the Riverfront Trail along the Colorado River in Grand Junction.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Part of the Riverfront Trail was closed for several days in Grand Junction as a result of flooding due to accelerated snowmelt. The trail at the Redlands Parkway underpass was under water, but the main road itself remained dry.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough tracked across the Northern Rockies, dragging its base across the Western Slope. This resulted in increased instability which fueled scattered showers. Showers were high-based given the abundance of dry air at the lower levels. Strong outflow from collapsing showers produced gusty winds in excess of 58 mph across portions of Montrose County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 62 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Montrose Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture increased across western Colorado as the next shortwave trough impacted the area. This, paired with daytime heating, led to scattered showers and thunderstorms. The stronger storms produced gusty winds in excess of 58 mph across some of the central and southern valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 76 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Durango-La Plata County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure dominated the Desert Southwest for several days before its axis moved directly over the Western Slope. This led to scorching heat across the desert valley floors under sunny skies with humidities dropping into the single digits to low teens.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures of 100 to 110 degrees were reported by various automated weather stations across the Grand Valley. The Grand Junction Regional Airport set a new record high temperature for the day of 107 degrees. This also tied the all time record high for the airport. Heat indices exceeded 100 degrees during the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture increased across western Colorado as the next shortwave trough impacted the area. This, paired with daytime heating, led to scattered showers and thunderstorms. The stronger storms produced gusty winds in excess of 58 mph across some of the central and southern valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 59 MPH was measured by an automated weather station 7 miles south-southwest of Olathe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accelerated snowmelt from a near-record snowpack due to above normal temperatures produced high and fast flow on drainages in several west-central counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three river rafters using inflatable canoes got caught up in swift water flows which were elevated due to recent warm weather and accelerated snowmelt. One rafter got separated from the group and was discovered downstream. CPR efforts were not successful and rafter passed away.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN EL PASO COUNTY / MONUMENT RIDGE / RAMPART RANGE BELOW 7500 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak wave train propagated over Colorado providing light to moderate snowfall over southern Colorado around Christmas time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 6 inches of snow that fell as observed by the COOP observer. Regional ASOS show 3 hours of 1/4 mile visibility and 30 mph wind gusts in conjunction with the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accelerated snowmelt from a near-record snowpack due to above normal temperatures and precipitation produced high and fast flow on drainages in several west-central counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A family rafting on the Colorado River became separated when their boat capsized due the fast and high flows as a result of recent snowmelt. First responders were able to locate all of the victims, but one minor was unresponsive to CPR.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding along the Yampa River resulted in the closure of the Deerlodge Park Campground within Dinosaur National Monument.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS BETWEEN 7500 & 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period with a long wave trough developed over Colorado at the beginning of December.  At the beginning of the events and enhanced jet streak oriented from the SW to the NE formed over southern Colorado, which produced High Wind Warning Criteria winds. The lower elevations did not see much for snow, at most 5 inches, but the mountains produced magnitudes of around 1 foot and more.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surface station 3CO4 4 S Weston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long duration severe weather event across the northeast plains, and most of the Denver metropolitan area beginning during the early afternoon hours.   Scores of  tornadoes occurred across Washington and Logan counties.  Several rounds of hail across the Denver metro, with ping pong ball sized hail in downtown, larger hail up to 2 inches in diameter at concert in Red Rocks Amphitheatre.  In all, seven patrons were hospitalized,  with 83 treated on scene.   A substantial number of attendees suffered welts, bruises and broken fingers trying to protect themselves.  Several suffered concussions as well.  A total of 33 tornadoes touched down in Washington County, with 3 reported in Logan County.  There were reports of at least one home damaged west of Sterling and one commercial building collapse in Sterling. The home was damaged by one of the tornadoes.||Significant flooding and flash flooding also occurred in portions of the plains, including Elbert, Arapahoe, Adams, Logan, Washington and Morgan counties. There were likely several rural roads that flooded due to the heavy rainfall.  There was a flood fatality just south of I-70 along Watkins Rd.  First responders found two stranded cars swept into a field, but its occupants had either gotten out or had been rescued. The third vehicle ended up west of Watkins and 6th Ave. in what is normally a dry creek bed. South Metro���s water rescue team was then called out to do a water rescue but that operation turned into a recovery by the following morning.  Due to flooding, Watkins Road was closed between Interstate 70 and Jewell Ave.  Interstate 70 was also closed for several hours until water could recede from flooded underpasses. Minor flooding also occurred in portions of the Denver and the surrounding metro area.  Baseball size hail was observed in Washington County.  In Clear Creek County, a thunderstorm wind gusts to 68 mph was recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in open country.  No damage was observed.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS BETWEEN 8500 & 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long and sharp trough passed over the region that brought a strong cold front with very cold air behind it. The cold air created an efficient environment for efficient snow production, leading to heavy snowfall accumulations across the eastern mountains and the I-25 corridor from El Paso County down to the New Mexico border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued very warm temperatures on an above normal snowpack resulted in excessive runoff leading to flooding across portions of west-central Colorado, particularly Gunnison County. Conditions were only exacerbated by heavy rain from thunderstorms in the days prior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kebler Pass sustained major damage due to excessive runoff, knocking out a significant portion of the road about halfway through the pass. Due to the road not being passable by vehicle, the pass will remain closed until repairs can be made and the road is rebuilt. There is no estimated time on when Kebler Pass will reopen.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The K2V5 AWOS station reported a 61 mph thunderstorm wind gust at its location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of June 23rd, severe to destructive thunderstorms developed within East-Central Colorado just south of Interstate 70 and progressed north-northeast through the region. These thunderstorms produced destructive hail up to baseballs in size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of hail ranging from nickel to half dollar size received at location. Time is radar estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and evening hours of June 12th, a line of thunderstorms followed by scattered showers moved eastward through East-Central Colorado. These showers and thunderstorms produced some locally heavy rain in the region which resulted in flash flooding in Kit Carson County that caused some road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received of County Road U closed between County Roads 9 and 10 due to flash flooding. Time is radar estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long duration severe weather event across the northeast plains, and most of the Denver metropolitan area beginning during the early afternoon hours.   Scores of  tornadoes occurred across Washington and Logan counties.  Several rounds of hail across the Denver metro, with ping pong ball sized hail in downtown, larger hail up to 2 inches in diameter at concert in Red Rocks Amphitheatre.  In all, seven patrons were hospitalized,  with 83 treated on scene.   A substantial number of attendees suffered welts, bruises and broken fingers trying to protect themselves.  Several suffered concussions as well.  A total of 33 tornadoes touched down in Washington County, with 3 reported in Logan County.  There were reports of at least one home damaged west of Sterling and one commercial building collapse in Sterling. The home was damaged by one of the tornadoes.||Significant flooding and flash flooding also occurred in portions of the plains, including Elbert, Arapahoe, Adams, Logan, Washington and Morgan counties. There were likely several rural roads that flooded due to the heavy rainfall.  There was a flood fatality just south of I-70 along Watkins Rd.  First responders found two stranded cars swept into a field, but its occupants had either gotten out or had been rescued. The third vehicle ended up west of Watkins and 6th Ave. in what is normally a dry creek bed. South Metro���s water rescue team was then called out to do a water rescue but that operation turned into a recovery by the following morning.  Due to flooding, Watkins Road was closed between Interstate 70 and Jewell Ave.  Interstate 70 was also closed for several hours until water could recede from flooded underpasses. Minor flooding also occurred in portions of the Denver and the surrounding metro area.  Baseball size hail was observed in Washington County.  In Clear Creek County, a thunderstorm wind gusts to 68 mph was recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down briefly in open country. No damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accelerated snowmelt from a near-record snowpack due to above normal temperatures and precipitation produced high and fast flow on drainages in several west-central counties. As a result, flooding compromised various low-lying roads in the counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt produced a significant washout on National Forest System Road (NFSR) #711-Dry Fork of the Minnesota Creek road located at mile post 5.5 in the Paonia Ranger District. The road was not passable and remained closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accelerated snowmelt from a near-record snowpack due to above normal temperatures produced high and fast flow on the Roaring Fork River. One such area was between Wingo and Snowmass, Colorado, where an individual drowned after accidentally falling into the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roaring Fork River ran high and fast as a result of accelerated snowmelt. Rescuers were unable to resuscitate a person they recovered from the river near the Lazy Glen subdivision after strong, runoff-fueled currents carried the victim downstream roughly 1 1/2 to 2 miles. It did not appear the person had been in a water vessel like a boat or raft. The incident had the makings of someone falling into the river from its banks, but that wasn���t a certainty. Age and gender of victim is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late afternoon of the 27th, a few storm cells began to pop up in eastern Colorado. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reports hail from 0.75 inches up to quarter size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very warm spring temperatures and an above normal snowpack resulted in excessive runoff leading to mud and debris flows across portions of west-central Colorado, as well as damage due to flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive spring runoff and debris backing up in culverts led to a large sinkhole developing on Highway 133 north of Paonia and south of Somerset, Colorado between mile markers 14 and 19. This resulted in the closure of the highway to allow emergency repairs to be made. The sinking soils were first noticed on April 29, 2023 with the eventual collapse of the highway occurring on May 2, 2023.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very warm spring temperatures and an above normal snowpack resulted in excessive runoff leading to mud and debris flows across portions of west-central Colorado, as well as damage due to flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Saturated soils from excessive runoff of melting snow lead to numerous mud and debris flows near Glenwood Springs, CO and the near the Hanging Lake Trail in Glenwood Canyon. The mud trapped a train traveling through the west side of Glenwood Springs with no injuries reported. Access to the Hanging Lake Trail was cutoff with roadway closures. Mud also slid off of Red Mountain south of Glenwood Springs, prompting the additional closures of some roadways and a private bridge was washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accelerated snowmelt from a near-record snowpack due to above normal temperatures produced high and fast flow on drainages in Routt County. As a result, flooding compromised a county road near Milner, CO.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures increased snowmelt and caused flooding on Routt County Road 44 between Highway 40 and Routt County Road 42. The road was closed for an extended period of time.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Increased flood waters due to rapid snowmelt led to water and debris across Fall Creek Road from County Road 56L to the Little Cone Ranch Subdivision. The road was closed for several hours.

In the early afternoon of the 16th, a system of thunderstorms formed in east central Colorado and progressed eastward. The strongest cells of this system remained south of I-70 through Colorado. Up to 1.75 inch diameter hail was reported from this system. Multiple reports of greater than 2 inches of rain were also reported with this system which caused flash flooding to occur in Cheyenne county. Another round of heavy rain occurred on the 17th which worsened the flooding that began on the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reports hail up to golf ball size.

On the evening of June 23rd, severe to destructive thunderstorms developed within East-Central Colorado just south of Interstate 70 and progressed north-northeast through the region. These thunderstorms produced destructive hail up to baseballs in size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated ping pong ball sized hail reported at location. Time is radar estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the early afternoon of the 16th, a system of thunderstorms formed in east central Colorado and progressed eastward. The strongest cells of this system remained south of I-70 through Colorado. Up to 1.75 inch diameter hail was reported from this system. Multiple reports of greater than 2 inches of rain were also reported with this system which caused flash flooding to occur in Cheyenne county. Another round of heavy rain occurred on the 17th which worsened the flooding that began on the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of pea to 0.75 inch hail.

Accelerated snowmelt from a near-record snowpack due to above normal temperatures and precipitation produced high and fast flow on drainages in several west-central counties. As a result, flooding led to some damage on some roads in Pitkin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accelerated snowmelt resulted in the development of a sinkhole on the edge of Brush Creek Road immediately downhill of Lower Kearns Road in Snowmass Village. The road was closed for a short while before being reopened a day or so later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began from unknown causes 21 miles south of Durango near Bondad.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Arkansas Loop Fire began in late June from unknown causes, 21 miles south of Durango, near Bondad. The fire grew to 127 acres before it was contained in mid July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire started from unknown causes 5 miles southwest of Parachute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Spring Creek Fire started in late June 5 miles southwest of Parachute, and grew to 3286 acres before it was finally contained in early September. The cause of the fire is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire 18 miles south of Pagosa Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coal Mine Fire started in late June from a lightning strike, 18 miles south of Pagosa Springs, and grew to 292 acres before it was contained in mid July.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chris Mountain Fire started in late June from a lightning strike, 12 miles west of Pagosa Springs, and grew to 511 acres before it was contained in late July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Residual moisture paired with orographic support and daytime heating led to scattered showers and thunderstorms over the San Juan Mountains. Some of the stronger storms in northern La Plata County produced strong winds that damaged cottonwood trees and some windows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated to be between 50 and 58 mph broke the branches off of several cottonwood trees at a CoCoRaHS observer's house. Additionally, a neighbors house had one of their windows damaged due to the branches being flown around.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough tracked across the Northern Rockies, dragging its base across the Western Slope. This resulted in increased instability which fueled scattered showers and thunderstorms. Convection was high-based given the abundance of dry air at the lower levels. Strong outflow from collapsing storms produced gusty winds which resulted in some damage across portions of Moffat County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm moved over rural portions of Moffat County. Gusty winds associated with the high-based storm produced significant wind damage across Black Mountain Road. Many trees fell across the road and stranded motorists. Some of the trees were 16 to 20 inches in diameter. No sensors nearby but wind gusts were estimated to be at least 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first push of monsoon moisture of the season resulted in widespread showers and thunderstorms across western Colorado. Storms were slow moving and produced heavy rain. As a result, localized debris flows occurred across the region, including in Pitkin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Debris of mud and rock fell across portions of the Rio Grande Trail outside of Aspen as a result of heavy rain from thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough acted on residual monsoon moisture to fuel scattered showers and thunderstorms across west-central Colorado. Some stratiform showers produced wind gusts in excess of 58 mph in Garfield County, which met high wind criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 59 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Rifle Garfield County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mother Nature supplied most of the fireworks for the Fourth of July as severe thunderstorms moved across the region during the late afternoon and evening.  Intense wind gusts, locally heavy rain and minor street flooding accompanied the storms.  Denver Fire performed a swift water rescue in the area of 38th Avenue and Fox Street, in the evening when a motorist became stranded in their vehicle.  Several fireworks shows were either cancelled or postponed due to safety concerns associated with the inclement weather.  Peak wind gusts included:  74 mph northwest of Red Feather Lakes, 70 mph just northeast of Kremmling, and 67 mph at Buckley AFB.  Several areas along the urban corridor picked up anywhere from 1 to 2 inches of rainfall.  At Denver International Airport, a storm produced 0.77 inch of rainfall along with a peak wind gust to 54 mph.

A cluster of storms developed off the Front Range moving east across the Eastern Colorado plains. The storms moved into Yuma County during the early evening hours producing thunderstorm wind gusts of 45 to 64 mph. The strongest gusts were observed near the community of Heartstrong and the city of Wray.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed via the Osmus Farm mesonet.

Severe thunderstorms produced large hail and intense winds over parts of the urban corridor and northeast plains and included:  Adams, Arapahoe, Denver, Elbert, Jefferson, Lincoln, Phillips and Weld counties.  The hail ranged in size from 1 to 2 inches in diameter, with peak wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph.  In addition, heavy rain flooded an underpass along Highway 36, with 3 feet of standing water which closed the roadway for several hours.
