Several rounds of hail across the Denver metro, with ping pong ball sized hail in downtown, larger hail up to 2 inches in diameter at concert in Red Rocks Amphitheatre.  In all, seven patrons were hospitalized,  with 83 treated on scene.   A substantial number of attendees suffered welts, bruises and broken fingers trying to protect themselves.  Several suffered concussions as well.  A total of 33 tornadoes touched down in Washington County, with 3 reported in Logan County.  There were reports of at least one home damaged west of Sterling and one commercial building collapse in Sterling. The home was damaged by one of the tornadoes.||Significant flooding and flash flooding also occurred in portions of the plains, including Elbert, Arapahoe, Adams, Logan, Washington and Morgan counties. There were likely several rural roads that flooded due to the heavy rainfall.  There was a flood fatality just south of I-70 along Watkins Rd.  First responders found two stranded cars swept into a field, but its occupants had either gotten out or had been rescued. The third vehicle ended up west of Watkins and 6th Ave. in what is normally a dry creek bed. South Metro���s water rescue team was then called out to do a water rescue but that operation turned into a recovery by the following morning.  Due to flooding, Watkins Road was closed between Interstate 70 and Jewell Ave.  Interstate 70 was also closed for several hours until water could recede from flooded underpasses. Minor flooding also occurred in portions of the Denver and the surrounding metro area.  Baseball size hail was observed in Washington County.  In Clear Creek County, a thunderstorm wind gusts to 68 mph was recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced large hail and damage winds across parts of Lincoln, Washington and Weld counties.  Significant crop damage was observed northwest of Stoneham in northeast Weld County.  In addition, the combination of hail and wind damaged trees and property.  The hail ranged in size from 1 to 3.25 inches in diameter, with peak wind gusts estimated up to 70 mph.  One brief tornado touched down in Lincoln County, but no damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system dropped into the Great Basin. This led to a tightening southwest gradient over western Colorado and breezy conditions. Additionally, moisture out ahead of the system produced isolated showers and thunderstorms which led to even gustier winds. Some outflow from decaying thunderstorms generated wind gusts in excess of 58 mph in portions of Garfield County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 59 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Rifle Garfield County Airport.

Scaffolding crashed down onto the building in Denver. The damage appeared to be linked to a high-wind event that caused the scaffolding to come crashing down, but the cause is being investigated.  According to the cidery, there were a few dozen people inside when the scaffolding crashed into the building. No one was hurt. There was damage to both the exterior and interior of the building and the cidery.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scaffolding crashed down onto the building located at 4100 Jason Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long duration severe weather event across the northeast plains, and most of the Denver metropolitan area beginning during the early afternoon hours.   Scores of  tornadoes occurred across Washington and Logan counties.  Several rounds of hail across the Denver metro, with ping pong ball sized hail in downtown, larger hail up to 2 inches in diameter at concert in Red Rocks Amphitheatre.  In all, seven patrons were hospitalized,  with 83 treated on scene.   A substantial number of attendees suffered welts, bruises and broken fingers trying to protect themselves.  Several suffered concussions as well.  A total of 33 tornadoes touched down in Washington County, with 3 reported in Logan County.  There were reports of at least one home damaged west of Sterling and one commercial building collapse in Sterling. The home was damaged by one of the tornadoes.||Significant flooding and flash flooding also occurred in portions of the plains, including Elbert, Arapahoe, Adams, Logan, Washington and Morgan counties. There were likely several rural roads that flooded due to the heavy rainfall.  There was a flood fatality just south of I-70 along Watkins Rd.  First responders found two stranded cars swept into a field, but its occupants had either gotten out or had been rescued. The third vehicle ended up west of Watkins and 6th Ave. in what is normally a dry creek bed. South Metro���s water rescue team was then called out to do a water rescue but that operation turned into a recovery by the following morning.  Due to flooding, Watkins Road was closed between Interstate 70 and Jewell Ave.  Interstate 70 was also closed for several hours until water could recede from flooded underpasses. Minor flooding also occurred in portions of the Denver and the surrounding metro area.  Baseball size hail was observed in Washington County.  In Clear Creek County, a thunderstorm wind gusts to 68 mph was recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire 12 miles west-southwest of Delta in the Dominguez Escalante National Conservation Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Mesa Fire started in late July from a lightning strike 12 miles west-southwest of Delta in the Dominguez Escalante National Conservation Area. The fire grew to 4,009 acres before it was contained in mid September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accelerated snowmelt from a near-record snowpack due to above normal temperatures and precipitation produced high and fast flow on drainages in several west-central counties. As a result, flooding compromised various low-lying roads in the counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accelerated snowmelt led to high rises in Roubideau Creek which subsequently led to the washout of portions of Roubideau Canyon Road which runs along the creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some rogue severe criteria winds developed over the eastern plains.

Numerous supercell thunderstorms moved across parts of Logan, Morgan, Phillips, Weld and Washington counties during the evening and overnight, dropping baseball size hail or larger on several communities. Towns that received very large hail (baseball size or larger) included:  Akron, Iliff, Brush, Fort Morgan, Haxtun, Fleming, and Otis.  In addition to the large hail, an 82 mph wind gust was reported 6 SE of Holyoke and 81 mph wind gust was measured 8 S of Holyoke.  One large tree was downed by the wind.  Numerous reports of broken windows to homes along with extensive car damage.  Two tornadoes briefly touched down, but no damage was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down briefly in open country.  No damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Residual moisture from an incredibly anomalously wet June continues to moisten the lower levels aiding in ample amounts of instability. The northwest flow pattern created a lot of shear, which led to prolonged 3 day severe weather event over adjacent and eastern plains of southern Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief tornado reported by a storm chaser 15 miles northeast of Kim.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accelerated snowmelt from a near-record snowpack due to above normal temperatures produced high and fast flow on drainages in Mesa County that stemmed from the Dolores River. One such area was West Creek near Gateway, where an individual drowned after  accidentally backing their ATV into the fast-flowing, flooded creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West Creek in Mesa County ran high and fast as a result of accelerated snowmelt resulting in the creek with flooding observed by local law enforcement. West Creek drains south into the Dolores River. A 94-year old man drowned after accidentally backing his ATV into West Creek and was swept downstream.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging wind gusts across portions of Adams, Denver, Elbert, Lincoln and Logan Counties.  The hail ranged in size from 1 to 2 inches in diameter.  Peak wind gusts up to around 70 mph were observed.  In Logan County, a spotter reported significant crop damage at their location.  The storms also caused tree damage as well as property damage to homes and vehicles.  In Elbert County, a greenhouse sustained damage and several chickens were killed.  In Lincoln County,  significant hail and wind damage was reported between Genoa and Arriba.   Several semi-trailers were flipped.  Several power poles also were also knocked down near the intersection of county roads 42 and 3P.  Heavy rain produced localized flash flooding in the Cameron Peak burn scar north of Glen Haven.

Severe thunderstorms produced heavy rain, large hail and damaging wind across parts of Boulder, Denver, Elbert, Larimer and Weld counties.  One tornado touched down briefly near Carr, but no damage was observed.  Hail up to half dollar size was observed, along with peak wind gusts around 60 mph.  Heavy rainfall, up to 3.64 inches,  produced flooding and flash flooding around Fort Collins and Livermore in Larimer County.  Vehicles were stuck in up to 3 feet of standing water.  Water from heavy rainfall filled the lagoon and surrounding retaining walls near the Lory Student Center on Colorado State University's main campus in Fort Collins.  At least four buildings on campus were damaged by floodwater, university officials said.  In the Cameron Peak burn area localized flash flooding was observed.  Buckhorn Road was impassable from 1 mile east of Moose Ridge Road to Forest Road 129.  In addition, multiple mudslides forced the closure of Highway 126 in Jefferson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Buckhorn Road was impassable from 1 mile east of Moose Ridge Road to Forest Road 129.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported quarter sized hail at location along with winds estimated at 45 to 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced heavy rain, large hail and damaging wind across parts of Boulder, Denver, Elbert, Larimer and Weld counties.  One tornado touched down briefly near Carr, but no damage was observed.  Hail up to half dollar size was observed, along with peak wind gusts around 60 mph.  Heavy rainfall, up to 3.64 inches,  produced flooding and flash flooding around Fort Collins and Livermore in Larimer County.  Vehicles were stuck in up to 3 feet of standing water.  Water from heavy rainfall filled the lagoon and surrounding retaining walls near the Lory Student Center on Colorado State University's main campus in Fort Collins.  At least four buildings on campus were damaged by floodwater, university officials said.  In the Cameron Peak burn area localized flash flooding was observed.  Buckhorn Road was impassable from 1 mile east of Moose Ridge Road to Forest Road 129.  In addition, multiple mudslides forced the closure of Highway 126 in Jefferson County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced damaging wind and hail over parts of Douglas, Elbert, Lincoln, Logan and Washington counties.  The largest hail occurred in Logan and Washington counties, where it ranged from from 2 1/2 to 4 inches in diameter.  In addition, two tornadoes briefly touched down in Washington County; one produced EF1 damage.  The tornado lifted a garage off its foundation anchoring, and shifted it. The garage door also imploded and caused some roof damage. Other damage on the farm included snapped trees and broken wood power poles. Some lesser damage was observed to some outbuildings.  Lastly, straight-line winds damaged the shingles of a manufactured home and flipped a small barn.

After high pressure dominated western Colorado for several days, moisture gradually increased ahead of an approaching system. This led to the development of isolated to scattered high-based thunderstorms. Given the dry air at the lower levels, gusty outflow winds were the primary threat from storms. The stronger storms produced gusty winds in excess of 58 mph across Moffat County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 60 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Craig���Moffat County Airport.

The one and only time this year when there was a proper monsoonal tap over southern Colorado. In this case, there was weak northweserly flow aloft, and southeasterly flow at the surface, which resulted in a unstable and high shear environment. There was almost a tornado that touched down, however, no reports of debris or damage were reported.

CZ_NAME: JEFFERSON & W DOUGLAS COUNTIES ABOVE 6000 FEET / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / NE PARK COUNTIES BELOW 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm system produced flooding and flash flooding across the urban corridor and northeast plains, with heavy snowfall in the higher mountains of the Front Range.  Storm total rainfall during the 3-day event ranged from 4 to 7 inches across the urban corridor and Palmer Divide, with 2 to 5 inches across the plains.  Additionally, from 1 to 2 feet of heavy wet snow occurred above 10 thousand feet.  The heaviest rainfall occurred over Arapahoe, Denver, Douglas, Elbert and southern Jefferson counties.  Consequently, swollen creeks and streams produced flooding and flash flooding.  Numerous roadways were damaged or completely washed out including access roads to Cherry Creek State Park and National Wildlife Refuge at the Rocky Mountain Arsenal.  Numerous hiking trails in Castlewood Canyon State Park were closed as they were underwater and impassable. Dozens of roads in Adams and Weld counties were hit by flooding and were closed due to damage.  In Denver, a 24-hr daily record of 2.92 inches occurred on the 11th, with a 3-day total of 4.40 inches from the 10th-12th.  Three men in the foothills of Douglas County had to be rescued when their vehicle got stuck in snow along Rampart Range Road in Pike National Forest.  The heavy snow toppled trees which blocked the road.  Rescuers had to use chainsaws to cut through the downed trees to reach the stranded men; the rescue took nearly 21 hours to complete.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three men in the foothills of Douglas County had to be rescued when their vehicle got stuck in snow along Rampart Range Road in Pike National Forest.  The heavy snow toppled trees which blocked the road.  Rescuers had to use chainsaws to cut through the downed trees to reach the stranded men; the rescue took nearly 21 hours to complete.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail shredded leaves, created dents in vehicle and broke vehicle windows. There were also broken house windows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced hail up to quarter size near Parker.  Brief heavy rainfall was also observed, with nearly one inch in less than one hour.  Lightning struck a home near 38th and Everett in Wheat Ridge.  Lightning blasted a hole in the roof as well as the ceiling inside.  Electrical outlets were damaged and one of the interior walls was charred. Severe thunderstorms produced quarter to half dollar size hail in Douglas County.  A tornado briefly touched down in open country in Lincoln County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning damaged a home in Wheat Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms developed southwest of Denver and pushed north-northeast through the metro area. The storms produced extensive hail, ranging from quarter to tennis ball size.  As the storms move northeast onto the plains, the hail increase in size from baseball to softball size.  In addition, the storm produced several tornadoes as they move across Morgan and Washington counties.   Southeast of Brush, an EF1 tornado touched down and caused extensive damage to a residence.  Part of the roof was torn off the residence. The nearby barn had a portion of its roof ripped off the building. Some wood storage buildings were destroyed and trees on the property were uprooted. The two elderly residence in the home at the time were not injured.  Several other weaker tornadoes touched down in Washington County but no damage was observed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area (includes CoCoRaHS).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of dry and clear weather followed by two snow storms in late November created a snowpack capable of causing Small to Large (up to D2 in size) avalanches across western Colorado. Avalanche Danger in the area at the time of the accident was rated Considerable (3/5) above treeline and Moderate (2/5) below treeline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An avalanche caught four backcountry recreationists, all skiers, in a north-facing bowl on Marble Peak, south of the town of Marble, an area known locally as The Fridge. All 4 were caught in the avalanche, 3 were partially buried while 1 was able to ski out. 1 member of the group was injured in the avalanche.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm system produced flooding and flash flooding across the urban corridor and northeast plains, with heavy snowfall in the higher mountains of the Front Range.  Storm total rainfall during the 3-day event ranged from 4 to 7 inches across the urban corridor and Palmer Divide, with 2 to 5 inches across the plains.  Additionally, from 1 to 2 feet of heavy wet snow occurred above 10 thousand feet.  The heaviest rainfall occurred over Arapahoe, Denver, Douglas, Elbert and southern Jefferson counties.  Consequently, swollen creeks and streams produced flooding and flash flooding.  Numerous roadways were damaged or completely washed out including access roads to Cherry Creek State Park and National Wildlife Refuge at the Rocky Mountain Arsenal.  Numerous hiking trails in Castlewood Canyon State Park were closed as they were underwater and impassable. Dozens of roads in Adams and Weld counties were hit by flooding and were closed due to damage.  In Denver, a 24-hr daily record of 2.92 inches occurred on the 11th, with a 3-day total of 4.40 inches from the 10th-12th.  Three men in the foothills of Douglas County had to be rescued when their vehicle got stuck in snow along Rampart Range Road in Pike National Forest.  The heavy snow toppled trees which blocked the road.  Rescuers had to use chainsaws to cut through the downed trees to reach the stranded men; the rescue took nearly 21 hours to complete.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weld County closed 23 sections of county roads due to flooding.  Roads that were closed or damaged included:  Weld County Road 4 from County Roads 15-17, Weld County Road 6 from County Road 23-U.S. 85, Weld County Road 8 from County Road 23-U.S. 85, Weld County Road 10 from CO 79 - County Road 71, Weld County Road 10, from County Road 81-83, Weld County Road 14 from County Road 85-87, Weld County Road 15, from County Road 2-4, Weld County Road 16 from County Road 71-73, Weld County Road 16 from County Road 73-77, Weld County Road 17 from County Road 34-38, Weld County Road 42 from County Road 45-47, Weld County Road 48 from County Road 43-45, Weld County Road 50 from County Road 59-61, Weld County Road 57  from County Road 76-78, Weld County Road 61, from U.S. 34-County Road 50, Weld County Road 63 from U.S. 34-County Road 52, Weld County Road 72 from County Road  33-35, and Weld County Road 74 from CO 392-County Road 79.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low propagated from SW CONUS and rapidly intensified over the southeast Colorado plains. A combination of strong lift due to the rapidly intensifying cyclone and orographic lift created heavy snow over the mountains. Since this was a late spring system with origins from the Pacific, the lower elevations were warm enough for heavy rainfall that ended up causing some flooding issues along the Fountain Creek in El Paso County and Pueblo County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 1 foot of standing water as reported via video from law enforcement and social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early afternoon a cluster of thunderstorms developed over Kit Carson County. As the storm approached Burlington, Colorado, an outflow boundary pushed out ahead of it, creating blowing dust conditions with near zero visibility in northeast Cheyenne County and likely southeast Kit Carson County. As the storm moved over Burlington it produced thunderstorm wind gusts of 61 mph. Small pea sized hail was also observed in and north of Burlington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received of near zero visibility on Highway 385 near the Cheyenne-Kit Carson county line due to dust picked up by a thunderstorm outflow boundary gusting an estimated at 55 mph. The storm was located over Burlington, Colorado in Kit Carson County at the time of the report. No reports of a dust storm were reported from Kit Carson County, but it is likely similar conditions continued along the highway and the outflow.

CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Pacific storm system produced bands of heavy snowfall across much of north central and northeast Colorado.  The combination of plentiful moisture and strong upper level support associated with the jet stream that stretched west to east across the state. Bands of locally heavy snow impacted areas in and along the I-70 corridor, from the mountains eastward onto the adjacent plains.  Over the Front Range mountains, foothills and Palmer Divide, storm totals ranged from 9 to 17 inches.  Across the urban corridor and northeast plains, storm totals ranged from 3 to 8 inches.  The official snowfall measurement at  Denver International Airport was 6.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included 13 inches, 3 miles south of Estes Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low pressure system pushed across eastern Utah into southern Colorado, bringing moderate to heavy snowfall to the eastern Utah mountains and central and southern mountains of western Colorado, impacting post-Thanksgiving travel. Heavy snow bands resulted in localized areas of heavier snowfall across the southern valleys and mountains as well as valleys of northwest Colorado. Winds gusted above 45 mph in the San Juans with a peak of 67 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 7 to 14 inches of snow fell across the area, with locally higher amount of 25 inches reported at Park Reservoir SNOTEL. Winds less than 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants from Tropical Storm Harold lifted across the Desert Southwest. This, combined with already abundant subtropical moisture, resulted in widespread showers and thunderstorms across western Colorado. Storms were slow moving and produced heavy rain. As a result of the heavy rain, some flooding and debris flows occurred in portions of Gunnison, Garfield, Mesa, and Montezuma counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms resulted in minor flooding/ponding of water on portions of I-70 near mile marker 56 going east towards De Beque.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported several inches of rain running through the streets of Cortez as a result of heavy rain from thunderstorms. The Cooperative Observer measured 0.55 inches of rain in 35 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level high pressure center was located over central Arizona and central New Mexico which resulted in a favorable pattern for efficient diurnal wind fluctuations including upslope oriented winds towards WFO Pueblo's eastern mountains. An upper level high pressure center was located over central Arizona and central New Mexico which resulted in a favorable pattern for efficient diurnal wind fluctuations including upslope oriented winds towards WFO Pueblo's eastern mountains. Weak waves embedded in the upper level northwesterly flow enhanced the lift and the pattern enhanced the deep shear. The result was a very rare mountainous tornado that was observed at around 9,000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm formed in the higher terrain west of Denver and moved across the Arapahoe/Douglas County line, from the west metro Denver to south Metro Denver. Up to 2.5 inches hail occurred in the populated areas of Jefferson County. An EF1 tornado touched down just south of CO-470 in the city of Highlands Ranch. Preliminarily the tornado was on the ground for 6.3 miles.  No injuries were reported but over 100 trees were downed. Some window/shingle damage has been noted to both houses and commercial properties.  South Metro Fire Dapartment responded to dozens of calls in Highlands Ranch south of c470 between Lucent and Quebec for storm related damage including roof damage, trees down, natural gas leaks and electrical problems. |At Northridge Elementary School a tree had fallen on the school and portions of the roof were been ripped off. Valor Christian High School in Highlands Ranch also sustained damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail damaged a roof and window screens.

CZ_NAME: TELLER COUNTY / RAMPART RANGE ABOVE 7500 FT / PIKES PEAK BETWEEN 7500 & 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A positively tilted shortwave trough propagated over Colorado from November 8th - November 9th. Snowfall amounts were relatively low, but there were spot located in Fremont County that got around 8 of snow. This was also the first snowstorm of the year for much of the lower elevations. 1 to 3 of snow fell over the Colorado Springs metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 6 inches of snow that fell.

CZ_NAME: UPPER RIO GRANDE VALLEY / EASTERN SAN JUAN MOUNTAINS BELOW 10000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low propagates over southern Colorado, tightening near the Colorado and Kansas border. The cold air aloft behind the cyclone created efficient conditions for snow across the Pikes Peak region, with the heaviest snowfall falling over Teller County and extreme northwestern El Paso County and lower amounts over the lower elevations of El Paso County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 2.5 inches that fell over Creede, CO.

Numerous supercell thunderstorms moved across parts of Logan, Morgan, Phillips, Weld and Washington counties during the evening and overnight, dropping baseball size hail or larger on several communities. Towns that received very large hail (baseball size or larger) included:  Akron, Iliff, Brush, Fort Morgan, Haxtun, Fleming, and Otis.  In addition to the large hail, an 82 mph wind gust was reported 6 SE of Holyoke and 81 mph wind gust was measured 8 S of Holyoke.  One large tree was downed by the wind.  Numerous reports of broken windows to homes along with extensive car damage.  Two tornadoes briefly touched down, but no damage was observed.
A broad and large closed low propagated over the northern portion of Colorado bringing lighter snow across portions of southern Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 2 inches of snow that fell over Beulah, CO.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN EL PASO COUNTY / MONUMENT RIDGE / RAMPART RANGE BELOW 7500 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period with a long wave trough developed over Colorado at the beginning of December.  At the beginning of the events and enhanced jet streak oriented from the SW to the NE formed over southern Colorado, which produced High Wind Warning Criteria winds. The lower elevations did not see much for snow, at most 5 inches, but the mountains produced magnitudes of around 1 foot and more.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 2.5 inches of snow reported.

An upper level high pressure center was located over central Arizona and central New Mexico which resulted in a favorable pattern for efficient diurnal wind fluctuations including upslope oriented winds towards WFO Pueblo's eastern mountains. Weak waves embedded in the upper level northwesterly flow enhanced the lift and the pattern enhanced the deep shear. The result was a very rare mountainous tornado that was observed at around 9,000 feet.

Numerous supercell thunderstorms moved across parts of Logan, Morgan, Phillips, Weld and Washington counties during the evening and overnight, dropping baseball size hail or larger on several communities. Towns that received very large hail (baseball size or larger) included:  Akron, Iliff, Brush, Fort Morgan, Haxtun, Fleming, and Otis.  In addition to the large hail, an 82 mph wind gust was reported 6 SE of Holyoke and 81 mph wind gust was measured 8 S of Holyoke.  One large tree was downed by the wind.  Numerous reports of broken windows to homes along with extensive car damage.  Two tornadoes briefly touched down, but no damage was observed.

Long duration severe weather event across the northeast plains, and most of the Denver metropolitan area beginning during the early afternoon hours.   Scores of  tornadoes occurred across Washington and Logan counties.  Several rounds of hail across the Denver metro, with ping pong ball sized hail in downtown, larger hail up to 2 inches in diameter at concert in Red Rocks Amphitheatre.  In all, seven patrons were hospitalized,  with 83 treated on scene.   A substantial number of attendees suffered welts, bruises and broken fingers trying to protect themselves.  Several suffered concussions as well.  A total of 33 tornadoes touched down in Washington County, with 3 reported in Logan County.  There were reports of at least one home damaged west of Sterling and one commercial building collapse in Sterling. The home was damaged by one of the tornadoes.||Significant flooding and flash flooding also occurred in portions of the plains, including Elbert, Arapahoe, Adams, Logan, Washington and Morgan counties. There were likely several rural roads that flooded due to the heavy rainfall.  There was a flood fatality just south of I-70 along Watkins Rd.  First responders found two stranded cars swept into a field, but its occupants had either gotten out or had been rescued. The third vehicle ended up west of Watkins and 6th Ave. in what is normally a dry creek bed. South Metro���s water rescue team was then called out to do a water rescue but that operation turned into a recovery by the following morning.  Due to flooding, Watkins Road was closed between Interstate 70 and Jewell Ave.  Interstate 70 was also closed for several hours until water could recede from flooded underpasses. Minor flooding also occurred in portions of the Denver and the surrounding metro area.  Baseball size hail was observed in Washington County.  In Clear Creek County, a thunderstorm wind gusts to 68 mph was recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down briefly in open country. No damage was observed.

Enhanced wind shear due from the upper level northwesterly flow created an environment conducive to severe thunderstorms.

Numerous supercell thunderstorms moved across parts of Logan, Morgan, Phillips, Weld and Washington counties during the evening and overnight, dropping baseball size hail or larger on several communities. Towns that received very large hail (baseball size or larger) included:  Akron, Iliff, Brush, Fort Morgan, Haxtun, Fleming, and Otis.  In addition to the large hail, an 82 mph wind gust was reported 6 SE of Holyoke and 81 mph wind gust was measured 8 S of Holyoke.  One large tree was downed by the wind.  Numerous reports of broken windows to homes along with extensive car damage.  Two tornadoes briefly touched down, but no damage was observed.

A supercell thunderstorm formed in the higher terrain west of Denver and moved across the Arapahoe/Douglas County line, from the west metro Denver to south Metro Denver. Up to 2.5 inches hail occurred in the populated areas of Jefferson County. An EF1 tornado touched down just south of CO-470 in the city of Highlands Ranch. Preliminarily the tornado was on the ground for 6.3 miles.  No injuries were reported but over 100 trees were downed. Some window/shingle damage has been noted to both houses and commercial properties.  South Metro Fire Dapartment responded to dozens of calls in Highlands Ranch south of c470 between Lucent and Quebec for storm related damage including roof damage, trees down, natural gas leaks and electrical problems. |At Northridge Elementary School a tree had fallen on the school and portions of the roof were been ripped off. Valor Christian High School in Highlands Ranch also sustained damage.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN EL PASO COUNTY / MONUMENT RIDGE / RAMPART RANGE BELOW 7500 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low propagates over southern Colorado, tightening near the Colorado and Kansas border. The cold air aloft behind the cyclone created efficient conditions for snow across the Pikes Peak region, with the heaviest snowfall falling over Teller County and extreme northwestern El Paso County and lower amounts over the lower elevations of El Paso County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 2.0 inches of snow 2 miles north of Black Forest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level high pressure center was located over central Arizona and central New Mexico which resulted in a favorable pattern for efficient diurnal wind fluctuations including upslope oriented winds towards WFO Pueblo's eastern mountains. Weak waves embedded in the upper level northwesterly flow enhanced the lift and the pattern enhanced the deep shear. The result was a very rare mountainous tornado that was observed at around 9,000 feet.

CZ_NAME: COLORADO SPRINGS VICINITY / SOUTHERN EL PASO COUNTY / RAMPART RANGE BELOW 7500 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period with a long wave trough developed over Colorado at the beginning of December.  At the beginning of the events and enhanced jet streak oriented from the SW to the NE formed over southern Colorado, which produced High Wind Warning Criteria winds. The lower elevations did not see much for snow, at most 5 inches, but the mountains produced magnitudes of around 1 foot and more.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 4 inches of snow that fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long duration severe weather event across the northeast plains, and most of the Denver metropolitan area beginning during the early afternoon hours.   Scores of  tornadoes occurred across Washington and Logan counties.  Several rounds of hail across the Denver metro, with ping pong ball sized hail in downtown, larger hail up to 2 inches in diameter at concert in Red Rocks Amphitheatre.  In all, seven patrons were hospitalized,  with 83 treated on scene.   A substantial number of attendees suffered welts, bruises and broken fingers trying to protect themselves.  Several suffered concussions as well.  A total of 33 tornadoes touched down in Washington County, with 3 reported in Logan County.  There were reports of at least one home damaged west of Sterling and one commercial building collapse in Sterling. The home was damaged by one of the tornadoes.||Significant flooding and flash flooding also occurred in portions of the plains, including Elbert, Arapahoe, Adams, Logan, Washington and Morgan counties. There were likely several rural roads that flooded due to the heavy rainfall.  There was a flood fatality just south of I-70 along Watkins Rd.  First responders found two stranded cars swept into a field, but its occupants had either gotten out or had been rescued. The third vehicle ended up west of Watkins and 6th Ave. in what is normally a dry creek bed. South Metro���s water rescue team was then called out to do a water rescue but that operation turned into a recovery by the following morning.  Due to flooding, Watkins Road was closed between Interstate 70 and Jewell Ave.  Interstate 70 was also closed for several hours until water could recede from flooded underpasses. Minor flooding also occurred in portions of the Denver and the surrounding metro area.  Baseball size hail was observed in Washington County.  In Clear Creek County, a thunderstorm wind gusts to 68 mph was recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down briefly in open country. No damage was observed.

The second in a series of low pressure troughs moved across northwest and west-central Colorado, bringing heavy snowfall and strong winds to the northern and central Colorado mountains. This northwest flow event was also accompanied by a moderate to strong Atmospheric River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 2 to 5 inches of snow fell across the area with higher amounts of 5 to 7 inches of snow on the east side of the zone near Hayden. Winds gusted above 30 mph with a peak gust to 41 mph at the Hayden-Yampa Valley Regional Airport and the Craig-Moffat County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second in a series of low pressure troughs moved across northwest and west-central Colorado, bringing heavy snowfall and strong winds to the northern and central Colorado mountains. This northwest flow event was also accompanied by a moderate to strong Atmospheric River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 20 to 40 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of 68.6 inches of snow at Tower SNOTEL. Winds gusted above 45 mph on 12/2 to 12/4 with a peak gust of 70 mph at Storm Peak Lab on 12/3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a less impactful snow system that occurred the weekend before the Christmas holiday, another system formed in South Central/Southwestern Nebraska. This system moved southwest into Eastern Colorado producing blizzard conditions on the 25th and early on the 26th. The Burlington ASOS recorded 3.5 consecutive hours of blizzard conditions on the morning of the 26th. There were other times that conditions briefly met blizzard status, which prolonged the impact of this system. The snow, combined with wind gusts generally ranging from 30 to 45 mph with occasional gusts around 50 mph, caused blowing snow to reduce visibilities across portions of Eastern Colorado, heavily impacting travel in the area. Up to 6 inches of snow was reported in Eastern Colorado from this system. The storm finally left the area the morning of the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer reported 6 inches of new snowfall late in the morning on the 27th. Gusty winds lead to blowing snow that created brief blizzard conditions. The Yuma AWOS reported 36 mph gusts with 0.25 mile visibility. Gusts up to 53 mph were also reported during this system.||Key impacts:  limited visibility, snow packed roads, blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous supercell thunderstorms moved across parts of Logan, Morgan, Phillips, Weld and Washington counties during the evening and overnight, dropping baseball size hail or larger on several communities. Towns that received very large hail (baseball size or larger) included:  Akron, Iliff, Brush, Fort Morgan, Haxtun, Fleming, and Otis.  In addition to the large hail, an 82 mph wind gust was reported 6 SE of Holyoke and 81 mph wind gust was measured 8 S of Holyoke.  One large tree was downed by the wind.  Numerous reports of broken windows to homes along with extensive car damage.  Two tornadoes briefly touched down, but no damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough with an associated cold front pushed through western Colorado and produced moderate snow across the northern and central mountains and some valleys. Additionally, gusty winds occurred with winds exceeding 50 mph in the mountains at times and in excess of 40 mph in the valleys, resulting in reduced visibilities. Instability associated with the front led to stronger snow bands along portions of west-central Colorado. As a result, a long-lived snow squall impacted the I-70 corridor from Grand Junction east towards Vail as visibilities dropped to less than one quarter mile, also resulting in a 21 car pileup between DeBeque and Parachute and a 13 mile stretch of I-70 closed for much of the afternoon into the evening on 12/8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 7 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts up to 15.4 inches at Rabbit Ears Pass. Sustained winds were above 20 mph with gusts above 45 mph and up to 57 mph at times during the first 8 hours of this event on 12/7 and below 40 mph afterwards.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow squall moved through the area, dropping visibilities at the Grand Junction Regional Airport to half a mile or less as winds gusted above 30 mph peaking at 39 mph likely with the squall. Snowfall ranged from trace to 2 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 2 to 4 inches fell across the area. Additionally, a snow squall moved through the area on 12/8, dropping visibilities at the Eagle County Regional Airport to a quarter of a mile as winds gusted above 30 mph peaking at 42 mph at No Name Tunnel sensor likely with the squall. Glenwood Canyon closed shortly after the squall due to an accident with numerous accidents in Silt, Glenwood and Carbondale with roads quickly becoming snow covered.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough moved through the Four Corners region from Southern California, bringing moderate to heavy snow to the mountains of southeast Utah and southwest Colorado as well as the northwest Colorado mountains and valleys as cold air moved in on the backside of the system. This storm came in the days leading up to Christmas, affecting pre holiday travel.  The mild nature of this system limited snow accumulation in the southern valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds were generally below 25 mph on average with a peak gust of 34 mph at Lodore.

A low pressure trough with an associated cold front pushed through western Colorado and produced moderate snow across the northern and central mountains and some valleys. Additionally, gusty winds occurred with winds exceeding 50 mph in the mountains at times and in excess of 40 mph in the valleys, resulting in reduced visibilities. Instability associated with the front led to stronger snow bands along portions of west-central Colorado. As a result, a long-lived snow squall impacted the I-70 corridor from Grand Junction east towards Vail as visibilities dropped to less than one quarter mile, also resulting in a 21 car pileup between DeBeque and Parachute and a 13 mile stretch of I-70 closed for much of the afternoon into the evening on 12/8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 2 to 4 inches of snow fell across the area. Additionally, a snow squall moved through the area on 12/8, dropping visibilities at the Rifle Garfield County Airport to a quarter of a mile as winds gusted above 30 mph at times throughout the storm. This snow squall resulted in a 21 car pileup along I-70 between DeBeque and Parachute, causing a 13 mile stretch of I-70 to be closed for much of the afternoon and evening.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog was observed across the Animas River Basin, including at the Durango - La Plata County Airport where visibility was reduced to less than one quarter of a mile for several hours. In addition, several Colorado Department of Transportation road cameras were used to determine the extent of dense fog along the US Highway 160 corridor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous supercell thunderstorms moved across parts of Logan, Morgan, Phillips, Weld and Washington counties during the evening and overnight, dropping baseball size hail or larger on several communities. Towns that received very large hail (baseball size or larger) included:  Akron, Iliff, Brush, Fort Morgan, Haxtun, Fleming, and Otis.  In addition to the large hail, an 82 mph wind gust was reported 6 SE of Holyoke and 81 mph wind gust was measured 8 S of Holyoke.  One large tree was downed by the wind.  Numerous reports of broken windows to homes along with extensive car damage.  Two tornadoes briefly touched down, but no damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow, high winds and blowing snow produced blizzard conditions across the Palmer Divide and northeast plains.  Peak wind gusts from 50 mph to 66 mph were observed.  Storm totals anywhere from 5 to 12.5 inches occurred in and near the Palmer Divide and over the far northeast plains, with 1 to 4 inches elsewhere.  Snow drifts up to 18 inches deep were reported.  Numerous roads and highways were closed including:  Interstate 70 east of Denver, US-6, US-24, US34 (from Brush to the Nebraska state line), US-36 (from Last Chance to the Kansas state line), US-385, as well as several other smaller county roadways.  Peak wind gusts included:  66 mph, 8 miles north-northwest of Boyero, 64 mph at Denver International Airport, 63 mph Pawnee Buttes, 61 mph at Limon Municipal Airport, 60 mph near Agate and Cedar Point, and 58 mph near Bennett.

Long duration severe weather event across the northeast plains, and most of the Denver metropolitan area beginning during the early afternoon hours.   Scores of  tornadoes occurred across Washington and Logan counties.  Several rounds of hail across the Denver metro, with ping pong ball sized hail in downtown, larger hail up to 2 inches in diameter at concert in Red Rocks Amphitheatre.  In all, seven patrons were hospitalized,  with 83 treated on scene.   A substantial number of attendees suffered welts, bruises and broken fingers trying to protect themselves.  Several suffered concussions as well.  A total of 33 tornadoes touched down in Washington County, with 3 reported in Logan County.  There were reports of at least one home damaged west of Sterling and one commercial building collapse in Sterling. The home was damaged by one of the tornadoes.||Significant flooding and flash flooding also occurred in portions of the plains, including Elbert, Arapahoe, Adams, Logan, Washington and Morgan counties. There were likely several rural roads that flooded due to the heavy rainfall.  There was a flood fatality just south of I-70 along Watkins Rd.  First responders found two stranded cars swept into a field, but its occupants had either gotten out or had been rescued. The third vehicle ended up west of Watkins and 6th Ave. in what is normally a dry creek bed. South Metro���s water rescue team was then called out to do a water rescue but that operation turned into a recovery by the following morning.  Due to flooding, Watkins Road was closed between Interstate 70 and Jewell Ave.  Interstate 70 was also closed for several hours until water could recede from flooded underpasses. Minor flooding also occurred in portions of the Denver and the surrounding metro area.  Baseball size hail was observed in Washington County.  In Clear Creek County, a thunderstorm wind gusts to 68 mph was recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down briefly in open country. No damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of strong winds, and heavy snow occurred across the mountains and higher foothills of northern Colorado.  Peak wind gusts to 93 mph were recorded at the NCAR Mesa Lab in the foothills 2 miles south-southwest of Boulder, just ahead of the approaching winter storm.  In the Park and Gore Mountain ranges, SNOTEL sites over a 48-hr period, recorded storm totals from 35 to 49 inches!  Elsewhere, storm totals from 1 to 2 feet were more common.  In addition, extensive blowing snow from strong winds produced dangerous driving conditions over the higher mountain passes.  Some of the roads and highways were closed for a time due to the inclement weather. In and near the Front Range Foothills, peak wind gusts included:  93 mph at the NCAR Mesa Lab, 89 mph near Winter Park, 85 mph at the junction of highways 93 and 72, and 76 mph, 2 miles south of Boulder.

CZ_NAME: N DOUGLAS COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / DENVER / W ADAMS & ARAPAHOE COUNTIES / E BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow, high winds and blowing snow produced blizzard conditions across the Palmer Divide and northeast plains.  Peak wind gusts from 50 mph to 66 mph were observed.  Storm totals anywhere from 5 to 12.5 inches occurred in and near the Palmer Divide and over the far northeast plains, with 1 to 4 inches elsewhere.  Snow drifts up to 18 inches deep were reported.  Numerous roads and highways were closed including:  Interstate 70 east of Denver, US-6, US-24, US34 (from Brush to the Nebraska state line), US-36 (from Last Chance to the Kansas state line), US-385, as well as several other smaller county roadways.  Peak wind gusts included:  66 mph, 8 miles north-northwest of Boyero, 64 mph at Denver International Airport, 63 mph Pawnee Buttes, 61 mph at Limon Municipal Airport, 60 mph near Agate and Cedar Point, and 58 mph near Bennett.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds with light to moderate snowfall occurred over the southern and eastern parts of metro Denver.  Storm totals included 7.5 inches in southeast Aurora, 4 inches in east Denver, but just a trace of snow at Denver International Airport.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of strong winds, and heavy snow occurred across the mountains and higher foothills of northern Colorado.  Peak wind gusts to 93 mph were recorded at the NCAR Mesa Lab in the foothills 2 miles south-southwest of Boulder, just ahead of the approaching winter storm.  In the Park and Gore Mountain ranges, SNOTEL sites over a 48-hr period, recorded storm totals from 35 to 49 inches!  Elsewhere, storm totals from 1 to 2 feet were more common.  In addition, extensive blowing snow from strong winds produced dangerous driving conditions over the higher mountain passes.  Some of the roads and highways were closed for a time due to the inclement weather. In and near the Front Range Foothills, peak wind gusts included:  93 mph at the NCAR Mesa Lab, 89 mph near Winter Park, 85 mph at the junction of highways 93 and 72, and 76 mph, 2 miles south of Boulder.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  19.5 inches near Berthoud Pass, 16.5 inches near Loveland Pass, 13.5 inches near Copper Mountain,  5 miles west of Green Mountain Reservoir, and Lake Eldora.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long duration severe weather event across the northeast plains, and most of the Denver metropolitan area beginning during the early afternoon hours.   Scores of  tornadoes occurred across Washington and Logan counties.  Several rounds of hail across the Denver metro, with ping pong ball sized hail in downtown, larger hail up to 2 inches in diameter at concert in Red Rocks Amphitheatre.  In all, seven patrons were hospitalized,  with 83 treated on scene.   A substantial number of attendees suffered welts, bruises and broken fingers trying to protect themselves.  Several suffered concussions as well.  A total of 33 tornadoes touched down in Washington County, with 3 reported in Logan County.  There were reports of at least one home damaged west of Sterling and one commercial building collapse in Sterling. The home was damaged by one of the tornadoes.||Significant flooding and flash flooding also occurred in portions of the plains, including Elbert, Arapahoe, Adams, Logan, Washington and Morgan counties. There were likely several rural roads that flooded due to the heavy rainfall.  There was a flood fatality just south of I-70 along Watkins Rd.  First responders found two stranded cars swept into a field, but its occupants had either gotten out or had been rescued. The third vehicle ended up west of Watkins and 6th Ave. in what is normally a dry creek bed. South Metro���s water rescue team was then called out to do a water rescue but that operation turned into a recovery by the following morning.  Due to flooding, Watkins Road was closed between Interstate 70 and Jewell Ave.  Interstate 70 was also closed for several hours until water could recede from flooded underpasses. Minor flooding also occurred in portions of the Denver and the surrounding metro area.  Baseball size hail was observed in Washington County.  In Clear Creek County, a thunderstorm wind gusts to 68 mph was recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of torrential rain and hail caused flash flooding.  Multiple road closures were in effect due to washed out county roads, including County Road 17 south of CO 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain occurred across some areas of northeast Colorado from June 11th until the early hours of June 12th with flash flooding in some areas.  The worst areas were south and southeast of Fort Collins towards Timnath and Windsor and in the Milliken area.  Locations in these areas received from 3.0 to  5.5 inches of rain.  Another area was located in and near Limon where significant flash flooding occurred along with a few water rescues.  Several roads were closed due to flash flooding north of CO-86 and west of I-70. Rainfall amounts in the these areas ranged from 2.4 to 3.7 inches. Severe thunderstorms produced large hail, from quarter to ping pong ball size.  The hail piled anywhere from 4 to 8 inches deep along roads and highways in Elbert and Lincoln counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Exceptionally severe weather continues over southern Colorado with the continued deluge of moist air coming from the Pacific and remnant moisture from previous moisture from widespread heavy rainfall. The most notable weather feature was an EF3 tornado that hit a farm located in Prowers County. This was only the 3rd EF3 tornado recorded since NWS modernization in 1995.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief cone tornado formed for around 2 minutes. The tornado did hit a small fall located in northeastern El Paso County. Picture reveal minimal damage on weak structures. WCM called the farm to discuss damage with them and performed a virtual damage survey revealing EF0 damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and evening hours, scattered storms developed off the Palmer Divide and Front Range. These storms moved northeast across Eastern Colorado, evolving into a linear cluster that produced 50 to 70 mph wind gusts across Yuma County. The strongest winds were observed along and just south of Highway 34 from the city of Yuma to Wray. There was tree damage also reported southeast of Yuma where the strongest gusts occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large 8-inch diameter branches snapped off a tree at the location.

During the afternoon hours, a strong supercell developed over Logan and Washington Counties. As the storm moved southeast near the community of Hyde, Colorado, it produced a EF-3 tornado that continued into Yuma County. The tornado reached it's peak intensity while just southwest of the city of Yuma with significant damage to a residence and two metal buildings. The supercell continued to move south then southeast across Yuma County to produce a total of seven tornadoes, with one EF-2 tornado moving into far north Kit Carson County. The other tornadoes were ranked EF-U with another north of Abarr ranked an EF-0. The storm also produced thunderstorm wind gusts up to 100 mph with damage to trees and power poles. Large hail was another hazard produced with 1 to 5.25 inch hail reported across Yuma County. The 5.25 inch hailstone fell near Kirk, Colorado, and set a new hail size record for the state of Colorado.  After crossing into Kit Carson County the thunderstorm turned east, producing more severe weather in Sherman County Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media report with pictures of hail ranging from golf ball to tennis ball in size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed over parts of Douglas, Jefferson and Larimer counties.  The storms produced large hail ranging from nickel to quarter size.
Strong to severe thunderstorms developed over parts of Douglas, Jefferson and Larimer counties.  The storms produced large hail ranging from nickel to quarter size.

Long duration severe weather event across the northeast plains, and most of the Denver metropolitan area beginning during the early afternoon hours.   Scores of  tornadoes occurred across Washington and Logan counties.  Several rounds of hail across the Denver metro, with ping pong ball sized hail in downtown, larger hail up to 2 inches in diameter at concert in Red Rocks Amphitheatre.  In all, seven patrons were hospitalized,  with 83 treated on scene.   A substantial number of attendees suffered welts, bruises and broken fingers trying to protect themselves.  Several suffered concussions as well.  A total of 33 tornadoes touched down in Washington County, with 3 reported in Logan County.  There were reports of at least one home damaged west of Sterling and one commercial building collapse in Sterling. The home was damaged by one of the tornadoes.||Significant flooding and flash flooding also occurred in portions of the plains, including Elbert, Arapahoe, Adams, Logan, Washington and Morgan counties. There were likely several rural roads that flooded due to the heavy rainfall.  There was a flood fatality just south of I-70 along Watkins Rd.  First responders found two stranded cars swept into a field, but its occupants had either gotten out or had been rescued. The third vehicle ended up west of Watkins and 6th Ave. in what is normally a dry creek bed. South Metro���s water rescue team was then called out to do a water rescue but that operation turned into a recovery by the following morning.  Due to flooding, Watkins Road was closed between Interstate 70 and Jewell Ave.  Interstate 70 was also closed for several hours until water could recede from flooded underpasses. Minor flooding also occurred in portions of the Denver and the surrounding metro area.  Baseball size hail was observed in Washington County.  In Clear Creek County, a thunderstorm wind gusts to 68 mph was recorded.

Numerous supercell thunderstorms moved across parts of Logan, Morgan, Phillips, Weld and Washington counties during the evening and overnight, dropping baseball size hail or larger on several communities. Towns that received very large hail (baseball size or larger) included:  Akron, Iliff, Brush, Fort Morgan, Haxtun, Fleming, and Otis.  In addition to the large hail, an 82 mph wind gust was reported 6 SE of Holyoke and 81 mph wind gust was measured 8 S of Holyoke.  One large tree was downed by the wind.  Numerous reports of broken windows to homes along with extensive car damage.  Two tornadoes briefly touched down, but no damage was observed.

In the evening hours of the 4th, storms developed near the Panhandle of Nebraska, evolving into a line as it moved south-southeast. This line produced golf ball sized hail near Wray before moving into extreme Southwest Nebraska and Northwest Kansas. During the pre-dawn hours on the 5th, scattered storms developed off the Palmer Divide and moved east across southern Yuma County. This round of storms produced more golf ball hail and 60 mph wind gusts. A third wave of storms developed later in the morning, producing small hail up to penny size in Burlington, Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHs observer reported estimated golf ball sized hail with dents to vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours, a strong supercell developed over Logan and Washington Counties. As the storm moved southeast near the community of Hyde, Colorado, it produced a EF-3 tornado that continued into Yuma County. The tornado reached it's peak intensity while just southwest of the city of Yuma with significant damage to a residence and two metal buildings. The supercell continued to move south then southeast across Yuma County to produce a total of seven tornadoes, with one EF-2 tornado moving into far north Kit Carson County. The other tornadoes were ranked EF-U with another north of Abarr ranked an EF-0. The storm also produced thunderstorm wind gusts up to 100 mph with damage to trees and power poles. Large hail was another hazard produced with 1 to 5.25 inch hail reported across Yuma County. The 5.25 inch hailstone fell near Kirk, Colorado, and set a new hail size record for the state of Colorado.  After crossing into Kit Carson County the thunderstorm turned east, producing more severe weather in Sherman County Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A multivortex tornado was observed moving between County Roads 34 and 29 across open land. No damage was reported.

Very heavy rain occurred across some areas of northeast Colorado from June 11th until the early hours of June 12th with flash flooding in some areas.  The worst areas were south and southeast of Fort Collins towards Timnath and Windsor and in the Milliken area.  Locations in these areas received from 3.0 to  5.5 inches of rain.  Another area was located in and near Limon where significant flash flooding occurred along with a few water rescues.  Several roads were closed due to flash flooding north of CO-86 and west of I-70. Rainfall amounts in the these areas ranged from 2.4 to 3.7 inches. Severe thunderstorms produced large hail, from quarter to ping pong ball size.  The hail piled anywhere from 4 to 8 inches deep along roads and highways in Elbert and Lincoln counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and evening hours, scattered storms developed off the Palmer Divide and Front Range. These storms moved northeast across Eastern Colorado, evolving into a linear cluster that produced 50 to 70 mph wind gusts across Yuma County. The strongest winds were observed along and just south of Highway 34 from the city of Yuma to Wray. There was tree damage also reported southeast of Yuma where the strongest gusts occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather Underground mesonet station KCOYUMA15 at the RMR Ranch observed 59 to 65 mph gusts over a span of 11 minutes. The strongest gust occurred at 8:56 pm MDT.

During the afternoon hours, a strong supercell developed over Logan and Washington Counties. As the storm moved southeast near the community of Hyde, Colorado, it produced a EF-3 tornado that continued into Yuma County. The tornado reached it's peak intensity while just southwest of the city of Yuma with significant damage to a residence and two metal buildings. The supercell continued to move south then southeast across Yuma County to produce a total of seven tornadoes, with one EF-2 tornado moving into far north Kit Carson County. The other tornadoes were ranked EF-U with another north of Abarr ranked an EF-0. The storm also produced thunderstorm wind gusts up to 100 mph with damage to trees and power poles. Large hail was another hazard produced with 1 to 5.25 inch hail reported across Yuma County. The 5.25 inch hailstone fell near Kirk, Colorado, and set a new hail size record for the state of Colorado.  After crossing into Kit Carson County the thunderstorm turned east, producing more severe weather in Sherman County Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado remained in open fields, short of crossing County Road J. No damage was observed.

During the evening hours, scattered storms developed off the Colorado Front Range, moving east across the Plains. As the storms moved east, they congealed into a line that produced thunderstorm wind gusts of 40 to 69 mph in Cheyenne and Kit Carson counties. The strongest wind gusts of 69 mph were reported in Burlington, Colorado via the ASOS station.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Trained Spotter in Seibert estimated 60 mph wind gusts as a storm moved east.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced hail up to half dollar size and wind gusts around 60 mph across parts of Jefferson, Morgan. Washington and Weld counties.  In addition, localized flash flooding occurred in the the Cameron Peak burn scar.  Flash flooding was observed  |along the lower part of County Road 44H in Buckhorn Canyon , and on County Road 43 at Streamside Road east of Glen Haven.  Heavy rainfall also produced minor flooding between Pine and Buffalo Creek in southern Jefferson County.

Severe thunderstorms produced large hail, ranging from quarter to golfball size, across portions of Adams, Denver, Elbert, Larimer, Logan and Weld counties.  In addition, a few of the storms produced peak wind gusts from 64 to 68 mph.

Severe thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging straight-line winds over parts of Elbert, Lincoln, Weld and Washington counties.  The hail ranged in size from ping pong to tennis ball size.  In Weld County, intense straight-line winds downed two trees and flattened a nearby corn crop.
Strong to severe thunderstorms developed over parts of Douglas, Jefferson and Larimer counties.  The storms produced large hail ranging from nickel to quarter size.
A severe thunderstorm produced large hail up to half dollar size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail caused extensive leaf damage to an apple tree and garden.

The supercell continued to move south then southeast across Yuma County to produce a total of seven tornadoes, with one EF-2 tornado moving into far north Kit Carson County. The other tornadoes were ranked EF-U with another north of Abarr ranked an EF-0. The storm also produced thunderstorm wind gusts up to 100 mph with damage to trees and power poles. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began in far eastern Washington County near the town of Hyde and moved east-southeast into northwest Yuma County. In Yuma County, the tornado remained southwest of the city of Yuma, where it looped around three times along the path causing damage to irrigation pivots, trees, and numerous power poles. The tornado dissipated during a fourth large loop. There was also significant damage to a residential property, three grain bins, and a machine shed towards the end of the path. Image of the actual tornado path is attached.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long duration severe weather event across the northeast plains, and most of the Denver metropolitan area beginning during the early afternoon hours.   Scores of  tornadoes occurred across Washington and Logan counties.  Several rounds of hail across the Denver metro, with ping pong ball sized hail in downtown, larger hail up to 2 inches in diameter at concert in Red Rocks Amphitheatre.  In all, seven patrons were hospitalized,  with 83 treated on scene.   A substantial number of attendees suffered welts, bruises and broken fingers trying to protect themselves.  Several suffered concussions as well.  A total of 33 tornadoes touched down in Washington County, with 3 reported in Logan County.  There were reports of at least one home damaged west of Sterling and one commercial building collapse in Sterling. The home was damaged by one of the tornadoes.||Significant flooding and flash flooding also occurred in portions of the plains, including Elbert, Arapahoe, Adams, Logan, Washington and Morgan counties. There were likely several rural roads that flooded due to the heavy rainfall.  There was a flood fatality just south of I-70 along Watkins Rd.  First responders found two stranded cars swept into a field, but its occupants had either gotten out or had been rescued. The third vehicle ended up west of Watkins and 6th Ave. in what is normally a dry creek bed. South Metro���s water rescue team was then called out to do a water rescue but that operation turned into a recovery by the following morning.  Due to flooding, Watkins Road was closed between Interstate 70 and Jewell Ave.  Interstate 70 was also closed for several hours until water could recede from flooded underpasses. Minor flooding also occurred in portions of the Denver and the surrounding metro area.  Baseball size hail was observed in Washington County.  In Clear Creek County, a thunderstorm wind gusts to 68 mph was recorded.

During the evening hours, scattered storms developed off the Colorado Front Range, moving east across the Plains. As the storms moved east, they congealed into a line that produced thunderstorm wind gusts of 40 to 69 mph in Cheyenne and Kit Carson counties. The strongest wind gusts of 69 mph were reported in Burlington, Colorado via the ASOS station.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a max gust of 60.6 mph on their weather station.

Numerous supercell thunderstorms moved across parts of Logan, Morgan, Phillips, Weld and Washington counties during the evening and overnight, dropping baseball size hail or larger on several communities. Towns that received very large hail (baseball size or larger) included:  Akron, Iliff, Brush, Fort Morgan, Haxtun, Fleming, and Otis.  In addition to the large hail, an 82 mph wind gust was reported 6 SE of Holyoke and 81 mph wind gust was measured 8 S of Holyoke.  One large tree was downed by the wind.  Numerous reports of broken windows to homes along with extensive car damage.  Two tornadoes briefly touched down, but no damage was observed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received via social media of half dollar sized hail at location that stripped the corn in a field. Location and time is radar estimated.

Severe thunderstorms produced damaging wind and hail over parts of Douglas, Elbert, Lincoln, Logan and Washington counties.  The largest hail occurred in Logan and Washington counties, where it ranged from from 2 1/2 to 4 inches in diameter.  In addition, two tornadoes briefly touched down in Washington County; one produced EF1 damage.  The tornado lifted a garage off its foundation anchoring, and shifted it. The garage door also imploded and caused some roof damage. Other damage on the farm included snapped trees and broken wood power poles. Some lesser damage was observed to some outbuildings.  Lastly, straight-line winds damaged the shingles of a manufactured home and flipped a small barn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in an open field just southeast of Otis and continued to move east-southeast throughout eastern Washington County. As it approached County Road ZZ it lifted a garage off its foundation anchoring, and shifted it.  The garage door also imploded and about 10% of roof structure was lost. Other damage included snapped trees and broken wood power poles, with lesser damage to outbuildings. The tornado continued to track east-southeast into Yuma County.

During the early afternoon a cluster of thunderstorms developed over Kit Carson County. As the storm approached Burlington, Colorado, an outflow boundary pushed out ahead of it, creating blowing dust conditions with near zero visibility in northeast Cheyenne County and likely southeast Kit Carson County. As the storm moved over Burlington it produced thunderstorm wind gusts of 61 mph. Small pea sized hail was also observed in and north of Burlington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported via ASOS station KITR at the Burlington Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong cold front pushed into Western Colorado and brought moderate snowfall to elevations above 8500 feet in the northern and central mountains. Winds gusted up to 45 mph at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of close to 16 inches estimated at the Burro Mountain SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The continued deluge of heavy rain, severe thunderstorms, flash flooding, and tornadoes continue over southern Colorado by yet another closed low located over southern California. The largest hail stone was 4 inches as reported by law enforcement and emergency management located in Baca County. Heavy rains continue over the I-25 corridor, with multiple reports of additional flooding in El Paso and Pueblo Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported by Lamar Airport surface observation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another closed low pressure center formed over southern California advecting Pacific based moisture towards southern Colorado. There was a record 2 day amount of rain that fell at the City of Colorado Springs Airport. There was also a single day amount over 5 inches reported in El Paso County. Reports of flash flooding burdened El Paso, Pueblo, and Fremont Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 2 inches of rain reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Exceptionally severe weather continues over southern Colorado with the continued deluge of moist air coming from the Pacific and remnant moisture from previous moisture from widespread heavy rainfall. The most notable weather feature was an EF3 tornado that hit a farm located in Prowers County. This was only the 3rd EF3 tornado recorded since NWS modernization in 1995.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was also a second damaging tornado, rated EF-1, that occurred this day on June 23, 2023. This tornado drifted across mostly open rural land across far southern Baca County east of Campo, Colorado for most of its lifespan. At a point during the middle of the tornadoes life, it impacted an abandoned farm stead, damaging two structures on the property. After hitting this property, it continued to track eastward across County Road 43, where it knocked down wooden power poles. Also at this time, the tornado passed a couple hundred yards to the north of an occupied family farm, but no damage was reported at this location. After crossing County Road 43, the tornado drifted back into open countryside and began deviating to the NNE, where it eventually dissipated near County Road 45.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long duration severe weather event across the northeast plains, and most of the Denver metropolitan area beginning during the early afternoon hours.   Scores of  tornadoes occurred across Washington and Logan counties.  Several rounds of hail across the Denver metro, with ping pong ball sized hail in downtown, larger hail up to 2 inches in diameter at concert in Red Rocks Amphitheatre.  In all, seven patrons were hospitalized,  with 83 treated on scene.   A substantial number of attendees suffered welts, bruises and broken fingers trying to protect themselves.  Several suffered concussions as well.  A total of 33 tornadoes touched down in Washington County, with 3 reported in Logan County.  There were reports of at least one home damaged west of Sterling and one commercial building collapse in Sterling. The home was damaged by one of the tornadoes.||Significant flooding and flash flooding also occurred in portions of the plains, including Elbert, Arapahoe, Adams, Logan, Washington and Morgan counties. There were likely several rural roads that flooded due to the heavy rainfall.  There was a flood fatality just south of I-70 along Watkins Rd.  First responders found two stranded cars swept into a field, but its occupants had either gotten out or had been rescued. The third vehicle ended up west of Watkins and 6th Ave. in what is normally a dry creek bed. South Metro���s water rescue team was then called out to do a water rescue but that operation turned into a recovery by the following morning.  Due to flooding, Watkins Road was closed between Interstate 70 and Jewell Ave.  Interstate 70 was also closed for several hours until water could recede from flooded underpasses. Minor flooding also occurred in portions of the Denver and the surrounding metro area.  Baseball size hail was observed in Washington County.  In Clear Creek County, a thunderstorm wind gusts to 68 mph was recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous supercell thunderstorms moved across parts of Logan, Morgan, Phillips, Weld and Washington counties during the evening and overnight, dropping baseball size hail or larger on several communities. Numerous reports of broken windows to homes along with extensive car damage.  Two tornadoes briefly touched down, but no damage was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail broke windows, damaged vehicles and shredded trees.

Exceptionally severe weather continues over southern Colorado with the continued deluge of moist air coming from the Pacific and remnant moisture from previous moisture from widespread heavy rainfall. The most notable weather feature was an EF3 tornado that hit a farm located in Prowers County. This was only the 3rd EF3 tornado recorded since NWS modernization in 1995.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A well documented strong and long lasting EF-3 tornado meandered across Prowers County south of Granada, Colorado, on June 23, 2023, remaining over open countryside for most of its life. Early during the tornadoes lifespan, it heavily impacted a family farm. Two wooden barns were completely destroyed, with little evidence |remaining of them other than the outline where they once stood. A metal farm outbuilding was virtually gone, with only the twisted steel beam reinforcements remaining. The primary family house was completely destroyed, with only a part of an interior wall still in place. After impacting the family home, the tornado continued to the southeast and knocked down power lines along County Road 25 before heading back into open countryside and dissipating.

Long duration severe weather event across the northeast plains, and most of the Denver metropolitan area beginning during the early afternoon hours.   Scores of  tornadoes occurred across Washington and Logan counties.  Several rounds of hail across the Denver metro, with ping pong ball sized hail in downtown, larger hail up to 2 inches in diameter at concert in Red Rocks Amphitheatre.  In all, seven patrons were hospitalized,  with 83 treated on scene.   A substantial number of attendees suffered welts, bruises and broken fingers trying to protect themselves.  Several suffered concussions as well.  A total of 33 tornadoes touched down in Washington County, with 3 reported in Logan County.  There were reports of at least one home damaged west of Sterling and one commercial building collapse in Sterling. The home was damaged by one of the tornadoes.||Significant flooding and flash flooding also occurred in portions of the plains, including Elbert, Arapahoe, Adams, Logan, Washington and Morgan counties. There were likely several rural roads that flooded due to the heavy rainfall.  There was a flood fatality just south of I-70 along Watkins Rd.  First responders found two stranded cars swept into a field, but its occupants had either gotten out or had been rescued. The third vehicle ended up west of Watkins and 6th Ave. in what is normally a dry creek bed. South Metro���s water rescue team was then called out to do a water rescue but that operation turned into a recovery by the following morning.  Due to flooding, Watkins Road was closed between Interstate 70 and Jewell Ave.  Interstate 70 was also closed for several hours until water could recede from flooded underpasses. Minor flooding also occurred in portions of the Denver and the surrounding metro area.  Baseball size hail was observed in Washington County.  In Clear Creek County, a thunderstorm wind gusts to 68 mph was recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported that flooding forced the closure of CO-63 between Akron and Anton.  Rainfall totals ranged from 3 to 5 inches in the area.

Numerous supercell thunderstorms moved across parts of Logan, Morgan, Phillips, Weld and Washington counties during the evening and overnight, dropping baseball size hail or larger on several communities. Towns that received very large hail (baseball size or larger) included:  Akron, Iliff, Brush, Fort Morgan, Haxtun, Fleming, and Otis.  In addition to the large hail, an 82 mph wind gust was reported 6 SE of Holyoke and 81 mph wind gust was measured 8 S of Holyoke.  One large tree was downed by the wind.  Numerous reports of broken windows to homes along with extensive car damage.  Two tornadoes briefly touched down, but no damage was observed.
